name: Ransomware/Wiper on the Process Control Layer (DCS)
description: Ransomware or a wiper reaches the process control layer (DCS / Purdue Level 1-2 controllers and engineering workstations) after an IT-to-OT pivot, encrypting or bricking control logic and HMIs. Unlike a historian-only impact, this forces an uncontrolled or emergency trip of the physical process and an extended, manual restoration of controllers — the dominant cited financial risk for continuous-process manufacturers.
example_incidents: Norsk Hydro 2019 (LockerGoga; ~$75M total impact, multiple aluminum plants forced to manual operation); Colonial Pipeline 2021 (precautionary OT shutdown after IT ransomware, multi-day fuel disruption); Honda 2020 (Ekans/Snake, global plant stoppage).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's MALWARE/RANSOMWARE categories do not distinguish IT-data-encryption from control-layer impact that forces a physical-process trip. NOTE: this loss is bimodal — near-zero if the pivot is contained at the IT/OT boundary, vs catastrophic (uncontrolled trip + extended manual restoration) if it reaches controllers. A single PERT cannot represent that bimodality, so the tail metrics (VaR / expected shortfall) from this entry are indicative, not precise — treat as a starting calibration to tune.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: no citeable anchor (C-ii-b sweep) — analyst-judged PERT retained

name: Manipulation-of-View — Falsified Process Values
description: An adversary with OT foothold falsifies the data presented to operators — HMI readings, historian trends, alarm states — so the displayed process state diverges from physical reality (a 'manipulation of view'). Operators make decisions on bad data, or fail to react to a developing upset, while the manipulation persists undetected. This is the integrity-against-the-operator effect that ICS-targeting frameworks (Industroyer2, Pipedream/Incontroller) were built to support.
example_incidents: Ukraine 2015 grid attack (operators locked out / fed false HMI state while breakers were opened); Stuxnet 2010 (recorded-then-replayed sensor values masked centrifuge sabotage from operators).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype targets IT data integrity at rest; it does not model OT integrity-against-the-operator, where the loss is the consequence of decisions made on falsified live process state. Magnitude-shape note: a single unimodal PERT is appropriate here because loss accrues continuously with detection latency (how long operators act on bad data) rather than as a binary catastrophic trip — there is no near-zero-or-catastrophic discontinuity to model bimodally.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: Baseline: energy_utilities quantile_pair (p50=$146,000, p95=$3,000,000, sigma=1.8377081683) × multiplier=2.7. Derived p50=$394,200; supersedes prior per-scenario analyst-judged PERT anchor.

name: Field Instrument / Sensor Signal Spoofing (Purdue L0)
description: An adversary corrupts the integrity of Purdue Level 0/1 field signals — spoofing or biasing sensor inputs (flow, pressure, temperature, level) or actuator feedback so controllers and operators act on physically false measurements. The control loop is steered toward an out-of-spec state, or a real excursion is masked, while instrumentation appears nominal. Distinct from manipulation-of-view: the falsification is at the measurement source, below the HMI.
example_incidents: Stuxnet 2010 (manipulated centrifuge process parameters while feeding controllers prior recorded values); research demonstrations of analog sensor spoofing and false-data-injection against ICS measurement chains (e.g., academic FDI attacks on SCADA state estimation).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype does not capture Level-0 measurement-integrity attacks where falsified sensor signals drive the control loop itself. Magnitude-shape note: a single unimodal PERT fits because loss scales continuously with how far and how long the process is driven off-spec before the spoof is caught, not as a binary all-or-nothing safety trip.

name: Abused OEM / Integrator Remote-Maintenance Access
description: An attacker abuses standing OEM, integrator, or maintenance-vendor remote-access pathways into the OT environment (jump hosts, vendor VPNs, cellular service modems, remote-support tooling). These connections frequently bypass segmentation and carry privileged control access, so a compromised vendor credential or exposed service portal yields direct reach into controllers and HMIs, enabling disruptive commands or a hands-on-keyboard outage.
example_incidents: Oldsmar, FL water treatment 2021 (intrusion via remote-access software, attempt to alter sodium hydroxide setpoint); numerous Dragos/Claroty findings of internet-exposed vendor remote-access services into OT; CISA repeated advisories on exposed remote-access into water and energy OT.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR does not separately model OT remote-maintenance abuse, where trusted vendor pathways grant privileged control access that bypasses the segmentation FAIR's IT models assume, shortening the path from credential theft to physical disruption.

name: Power-Grid Protective Relay / IEC 61850 Manipulation
description: A nation-state actor manipulates electric-substation protection and control — issuing unauthorized breaker-open commands, abusing IEC 61850 / 60870-5-104 protocols, or disabling protective relays so faults are not cleared. The effect ranges from operator-visible load loss to equipment-damaging protection defeat, the signature capability demonstrated by the Industroyer/CRASHOVERRIDE and Industroyer2 toolsets against Ukrainian grid operators.
example_incidents: Ukraine 2016 (Industroyer/CRASHOVERRIDE, Kyiv substation de-energized via protocol abuse); Ukraine 2022 (Industroyer2 targeting a high-voltage substation, disrupted before full effect); Ukraine 2015 (manual breaker operation, ~225,000 customers lost power).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no archetype for electric-grid protection-system manipulation, where protocol-level commands to relays and breakers cause load loss or defeat fault clearing — a distinct frequency/magnitude profile (very low frequency, nation-state-only capability) that FAIR's generic denial-of-service does not represent.

name: Pipeline SCADA Pressure/Flow Integrity Attack
description: A nation-state actor with pipeline SCADA access manipulates the integrity of pressure, flow, and valve control — driving line pressure past safe limits, falsifying telemetry so leak-detection and operators stay blind, or mis-sequencing valves. The worst case is a physical pipeline rupture, release, or fire; the more common case is a forced shutdown once manipulation is suspected.
example_incidents: Colonial Pipeline 2021 (ransomware drove a precautionary shutdown, illustrating pipeline-SCADA fragility — not an integrity attack itself but the systemic-impact reference); CISA 2021-2022 warnings on Russian state targeting of U.S. pipeline OT; 2011-2013 GAO/ICS-CERT reporting on intrusions into U.S. natural-gas pipeline operators.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no archetype for pipeline SCADA integrity attacks where the primary loss is a physical release or rupture. NOTE: this loss is bimodal — near-zero if the manipulation is detected and the line is safely shut, vs catastrophic (rupture, release, fire, environmental and casualty liability) if pressure/flow control is defeated unchecked. A single PERT cannot represent that bimodality, so the tail metrics (VaR / expected shortfall) from this entry are indicative, not precise — treat as a starting calibration to tune.

name: Chemical Process Overpressure / Runaway via Control Manipulation
description: A nation-state actor manipulates basic process control and defeats or degrades the safety instrumented system (SIS) of a chemical or petrochemical process to drive an overpressure, exotherm/runaway reaction, or toxic release. This is the TRITON/TRISIS objective: with the SIS compromised, a deliberately induced process upset is no longer arrested by the last safety barrier, opening a catastrophic-consequence tail (explosion, toxic release, fatalities).
example_incidents: Triton/TRISIS 2017 (Triconex SIS at a Middle East petrochemical plant; attackers caused two trips before discovery — the attack failed before final physical consequence); historical (non-cyber) process-safety catastrophes (Bhopal, Flixborough) bound the physical-consequence magnitude such an attack targets.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no archetype for safety-layer attacks where the primary loss is the defeat of physical safety barriers and the resulting industrial catastrophe. NOTE: this loss is bimodal — near-zero if the attack fails pre-consequence (as TRITON did), vs catastrophic (explosion, toxic release, fatalities, losses that can exceed enterprise value) if a process upset occurs with the SIS defeated. A single PERT cannot represent that bimodality, so the tail metrics (VaR / expected shortfall) from this entry are indicative, not precise — treat as a starting calibration to tune.

name: Accidental Insider Data Exposure (Misdelivery / Misconfig)
description: A non-malicious insider exposes sensitive data through error — emailing a file to the wrong recipient, attaching the wrong document, mis-setting a SharePoint/cloud-share permission, leaving a database or mailbox publicly accessible, or mis-publishing a report. There is no adversary motive; the loss is driven by what was exposed and the resulting breach-notification and regulatory obligations. Miscellaneous errors (misdelivery, misconfiguration, publishing error) are consistently one of the largest breach-cause categories in industry reporting, yet are under-represented relative to attack-driven scenarios in this library.
example_incidents: Misdelivery (wrong-recipient email/fax) is a recurring top error pattern in Verizon DBIR breach data; municipal and healthcare mis-publishing incidents (public-facing portals exposing PII) reported routinely to HHS OCR and state AGs; cloud-share misconfiguration exposures of internal HR and customer files.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_DISCLOSURE / insider archetypes conflate malicious exfiltration with non-malicious error. Accidental exposure has no Threat Capability or motive term — vulnerability is effectively the probability that an error reaches sensitive data uncaught — so the frequency/magnitude profile (high event frequency, modest magnitude, regulatory-notification-driven secondary loss) differs materially from malicious-insider models and is not separately represented in the canonical taxonomy.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: no citeable anchor (C-ii-b sweep) — analyst-judged PERT retained

name: Web Application Exploitation — SQLi / API Abuse to Exfiltration
description: Cybercriminals exploit a vulnerability in an internet-facing web application or API — SQL injection, broken access control / IDOR, server-side request forgery, or an unauthenticated API endpoint — to read and exfiltrate the backing data store. Basic web-application attacks are the single largest pattern in external-breach data, and the loss is dominated by the volume and sensitivity of records exposed plus the downstream breach-notification and regulatory tail.
example_incidents: Capital One 2019 (SSRF against a web application reached IAM credentials and exfiltrated ~106M records; ~USD 270M total cost incl. USD 80M OCC fine); routine OWASP-class SQLi and broken-access-control breaches reported across financial, retail, and information sectors.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_DISCLOSURE archetype does not separate web-application-layer exploitation (where vulnerability is governed by application code quality and access-control design, and the threat is volumetric internet scanning) from network-perimeter or credential-driven disclosure. The vulnerability driver — exploitable application logic exposed to the entire internet — and the record-count-driven magnitude warrant a distinct calibration.

name: Third-Party / MSP Data-Processor Breach
description: A cybercriminal compromises a third party that processes or hosts the organization's data — a SaaS vendor, managed service provider (MSP), payroll/benefits processor, BPO, or law firm — and the organization's records are breached even though its own perimeter was never touched. The organization carries the notification, regulatory, and reputational liability for data held by the processor. MSP and processor compromises also amplify blast radius: one vendor breach simultaneously exposes many downstream customers.
example_incidents: Kaseya VSA 2021 (MSP RMM-tool compromise cascaded ransomware/exposure to ~1,500 downstream customers; REvil demanded USD 70M); MOVEit / Cl0p 2023 (zero-day in a widely used managed-file-transfer service breached 2,600+ organizations as a data-processor supply-chain event); routine cloud/SaaS processor breaches triggering downstream-customer notification.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's SUPPLY_CHAIN category centers on trojanized software updates and vendor-software backdoors; it does not cleanly model the data-processor case where the loss event occurs entirely within a third party's environment but the legal and reputational liability accrues to the data owner. The vulnerability term is the processor's security posture (largely outside the owner's direct control), which the canonical single-organization model does not represent.

name: Retail POS / E-commerce Card Skimming (Magecart)
description: Cybercriminals harvest payment-card data either by injecting malicious JavaScript into an e-commerce checkout page or a third-party script (digital skimming / Magecart formjacking) or by deploying memory-scraping malware on point-of-sale terminals. Captured card data is sold or used for fraud. Loss is driven by cards exposed, card-brand assessments, forensic and PCI-DSS remediation costs, and regulatory exposure, with a heavy secondary-loss tail for large retailers.
example_incidents: British Airways 2018 (Magecart skimming script on the booking flow exposed ~400,000 payment cards; UK ICO GDPR penalty of GBP 20M, reduced from an initial GBP 183M intent); Target 2013 (POS memory-scraping malware exposed ~40M payment cards; >USD 200M cumulative bank/legal costs); ongoing Magecart formjacking campaigns across e-commerce sites.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_DISCLOSURE archetype does not distinguish payment-card skimming, where the asset is cardholder data, the secondary loss includes card-brand assessments and PCI-DSS-driven forensic/remediation costs distinct from PII breach notification, and the threat operates either client-side (browser script injection) or on the POS terminal — vulnerability surfaces the generic model does not represent.

name: Public-Sector Targeted Intrusion / Municipal Ransomware
description: Cybercriminals compromise a state or local government / municipal environment — via phishing-derived credentials, exposed RDP, or unpatched perimeter services — escalate through a typically flat Active Directory, and deploy ransomware that halts citizen-facing and internal services (permitting, courts, billing, email, 911-adjacent systems). Public-sector bodies face elevated exposure from legacy systems, constrained security budgets, and the political pressure of restoring public services, while disclosed records add a breach-notification tail.
example_incidents: City of Atlanta 2018 (SamSam ransomware; recovery and rebuild costs reported in the tens of millions of dollars); City of Baltimore 2019 (RobbinHood ransomware; ~USD 18M+ in recovery and lost-revenue costs, weeks of degraded services); numerous county and municipal ransomware incidents tracked in CISA / MS-ISAC reporting.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's RANSOMWARE archetype does not capture the public-sector loss structure, where primary loss is the disruption of non-revenue citizen services (whose value is not a commercial productivity figure) plus rebuild costs of legacy systems, and the org-size/budget profile drives a higher vulnerability than the enterprise-calibrated baseline assumes. Magnitude is dominated by restoration and service-continuity cost rather than a ransom-payment or revenue-loss figure, and the backups-present vs. forced-rebuild outcome split is collapsed into a single right-skewed magnitude rather than modeled as a regime mixture.

name: Transportation & Logistics Operational Disruption
description: Ransomware or a destructive wiper disrupts the IT systems that run a transportation or logistics operator — terminal operating systems, booking and tracking platforms, warehouse-management and dispatch systems — halting the physical movement of freight, containers, or parcels. Unlike a data-centric breach, the dominant loss is operational: idled fleets and terminals, manual fallback processes, and contractual/SLA penalties cascading to downstream shippers, with recovery often spanning weeks across many sites.
example_incidents: Maersk / NotPetya 2017 (destructive wiper crippled global shipping and terminal operations; Maersk reported ~USD 300M impact and a near-total Active Directory rebuild); Expeditors International 2022 (ransomware forced a multi-week shutdown of global operations); ransomware against ports and trucking/3PL operators reported across the sector.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's RANSOMWARE / availability archetypes model primary loss as data recovery and internal business continuity; they do not represent the logistics case where the loss is physical-throughput disruption (freight not moving), SLA and demurrage penalties, and cascading liability to downstream shippers across many geographically distributed sites — a multi-site operational-throughput magnitude the single-site IT-continuity model under-states — and the backups-present vs. forced-rebuild outcome split is collapsed into a single right-skewed magnitude rather than modeled as a regime mixture.

name: Telecom Subscriber Data Breach — Mass PII Exfiltration
description: Cybercriminals exploit a vulnerability in a telecom carrier's customer-data platform, billing system, or identity-management infrastructure to exfiltrate subscriber records at scale — names, phone numbers, call records, device identifiers, and account credentials. The loss is driven by breach-notification obligations, FCC/FTC regulatory exposure, class-action settlements, and reputational churn, with the primary magnitude anchored to the IRIS Information-sector loss distribution.
example_incidents: T-Mobile 2021 (MVNO/prepaid portal exploitation exfiltrated ~76.6 M customer records; class-action settled for $350 M + $150 M security investment; FCC settled for $31.5 M in 2024). AT&T 2024 (call records for ~109 M customers exposed via third-party cloud platform).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_DISCLOSURE archetype does not capture the telecom carrier's subscriber-data profile: call detail records and SIM/device identifiers are distinct from enterprise PII, the FCC CPNI regulatory framework adds sector-specific secondary exposure beyond GDPR/CCPA, and aggregated subscriber counts drive magnitude non-linearly with breach scale.

name: Hospitality Loyalty Program Account Takeover — Points Fraud
description: Cybercriminals use credential stuffing, phishing, or SIM-swap attacks to take over hotel, airline, or rental-car loyalty accounts and redeem accumulated points or miles for cash-equivalent travel, gift cards, or merchandise. The carrier-side loss is a blend of fraudulent redemption write-offs, customer notification, identity-theft remediation, and brand damage, with a long tail from class-action settlements related to account security failures.
example_incidents: IHG/Holiday Inn loyalty program 2022 (credential-stuffing attack emptied loyalty points for many customers; acknowledged as a widespread attack wave). TEF is campaign-level: one credential-stuffing/ATO wave is one TEF event; per-account-attempt frequency is NOT the TEF unit.

Loss includes breach notification, PCI-DSS remediation, GDPR/CCPA regulatory fines, and litigation from impacted guests.

TEF is campaign-level: a Killnet-class campaign is one TEF event regardless of the packet-per-second rate.

name: Government Employee Insider Data Leak — Unauthorized Sensitive Disclosure
description: A malicious government employee deliberately exfiltrates and discloses sensitive or classified records — personnel files, law-enforcement databases, citizen benefit records, or national-security information — to external parties, media, or foreign actors. Loss is driven by investigation and remediation costs, harm to individuals whose records are disclosed (privacy liability), agency operational disruption, legal/IG proceedings, and downstream program costs if exposed information is acted upon adversarially.
example_incidents: Harold Martin (NSA contractor, 2016 — unauthorized removal of approximately 50 TB of classified material accumulated over two decades; DOJ prosecution and guilty plea). Reality Winner (NSA contractor, 2017 — deliberate disclosure of a classified signals-intelligence report to a media outlet; DOJ prosecution and conviction). Periodic additional DOJ/FBI contractor indictments for unauthorized removal or disclosure of sensitive government data disclosed on PACER.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's INSIDER_MISUSE archetype focuses on direct financial loss from unauthorized data access; government insider leaks also generate harm to individuals (clearance holders, informants, protected sources) whose safety may be jeopardized — a consequence class FAIR's financial-primary model does not represent, and the investigation/remediation secondary can exceed the primary when national-security programs are compromised.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Public sector pair (p50=$214,000, p95=$18,000,000); sigma=2.6945564938. secondary sigma inherited from primary; location scaled by analyst-judged secondary/primary ratio R=0.40 (government insider leak secondary: investigation + legal exposure + remediation + public communications ~40% of primary; insider disclosure triggers both legal and reputational secondary costs)

name: Manufacturing Competitor-Driven IP Theft — Trade Secret Exfiltration
description: A competitor recruits or compromises an insider to exfiltrate manufacturing trade secrets — proprietary process designs, chemical formulations, tooling specifications, supplier contracts, or customer pricing models. Unlike nation-state espionage, competitor-driven IP theft is aimed at shortcutting R&D investment and competing directly. Loss includes the economic value of the stolen IP (proxied via litigation awards), forensic investigation, trade-secret misappropriation litigation costs under the DTSA, and remediation of the compromised access pathways.
example_incidents: DuPont trade secret cases (multiple DOJ indictments for theft of manufacturing process IP by employees recruited by competitors or foreign state-linked entities). AGCO / CNH Industrial litigation (former employees transferred proprietary manufacturing data to competitors). Corning glass process IP cases.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)

LOSS UNDERSTATED: the dominant competitive-advantage / IP-theft loss channel is UNMODELED -- no defensible public magnitude source (adjudicated-damages sweep found none; the entry's prior IP cites are the gap-report mis-cites). Only incident-response + reputation are modeled here. Explicit sec6-materiality-bar waiver (plan-gate R11).
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's INSIDER_MISUSE archetype models individual-actor misuse; competitor-orchestrated IP theft involves a coordinating external actor (the competitor) not modeled as a FAIR threat community, and the primary loss is IP-economic-value destruction (requiring analyst substitution of litigation and competitive-harm proxies for the canonical loss form, since FAIR's LM does not directly model IP value).

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Manufacturing sector pair (p50=$1,000,000, p95=$42,000,000); sigma=2.2723417799. secondary sigma inherited from primary; location scaled by analyst-judged secondary/primary ratio R=0.15 (competitor IP-theft secondary: trade-secret litigation + brand harm + customer loss ~15% of primary; IP theft secondary is legal/reputational tail well below primary economic-value loss)

loss_form_profile.magnitude_basis: UNMODELED -- dominant IP/trade-secret loss, no defensible public magnitude source (beyond-envelope, sec6 waiver).

name: Manufacturing Insider Billing Fraud — Fictitious Vendor AP Manipulation
description: A malicious insider with access to the ERP or accounts-payable system creates fictitious vendor records, manipulates purchase orders, or diverts payments to accounts they control. Manufacturing organizations are particularly exposed because of complex supplier networks, multiple procurement systems (ERP + MES + supply-chain portals), and high transaction volumes that can mask fraudulent disbursements. Loss is direct financial theft plus investigation, audit remediation, and internal-controls strengthening costs.
example_incidents: Multiple manufacturing-sector AP fraud indictments in US DOJ press releases: fictitious-vendor schemes in auto parts, food processing, and chemical manufacturing totaling $1M–$10M per incident. ACFE Report to the Nations (biennial) consistently places manufacturing among the higher-median-loss sectors for asset misappropriation (see acfe.com/report-to-the-nations; specific figure not page-cited here).
source_citations: IC3 2025 Annual Report p.7-8, p.132 (BEC per-complaint mean $123,005; downward-biased -- mixes consumer/SMB with corporate)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's INSIDER_MISUSE archetype does not distinguish AP/billing fraud, where the primary loss is direct theft of cash via manipulated ERP disbursements (distinct from data exfiltration), and the vulnerability depends on financial-control weaknesses (segregation-of-duties gaps, approval-authority gaps) rather than data-access controls — requiring different control-mapping than the data-theft model.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Manufacturing sector pair (p50=$1,000,000, p95=$42,000,000); sigma=2.2723417799. secondary sigma inherited from primary; location scaled by analyst-judged secondary/primary ratio R=0.25 (billing fraud secondary: audit remediation + regulatory reporting + controls enhancement ~25% of primary; insider billing fraud secondary bounded by audit and governance remediation costs)

loss_form_profile.magnitude_basis: IC3 2025 BEC-specific per-complaint mean $123,005 (beyond-envelope own distribution; funds-transfer loss). Mean-preserving lognormal: mu = ln(mean) - sigma^2/2 so E[loss] = mean exactly; median = mean*exp(-sigma^2/2). sigma borrowed from the manufacturing envelope (disclosed shape assumption, A1 parity).

name: Healthcare Staff Credential Phishing — PHI Breach via Account Compromise
description: Cybercriminals send spear-phishing emails targeting clinical and administrative healthcare staff to capture EHR login credentials. Once compromised, the attacker accesses electronic health records, exfiltrates protected health information (PHI) for sale on dark-web markets, or uses the account for business-email compromise against the organization's vendors. HIPAA breach notification, HHS OCR investigation, and class-action settlement drive the loss, with a secondary tail from workforce disruption and controls remediation.
example_incidents: CommonSpirit Health 2022 (ransomware post-phishing; ~$150 M total impact including prolonged EHR downtime). Scripps Health 2021 (phishing-to-ransomware; $113 M disclosed cost). Routine BEC via staff phishing reported quarterly in HHS OCR breach portal (healthcare routinely accounts for the largest number of HIPAA breach notifications).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's SOCIAL_ENGINEERING archetype does not represent the HIPAA-specific regulatory secondary, where per-affected-individual notification cost and HHS OCR tiered-penalty structure (up to $2.13 M/year per violation category) amplify secondary loss well above a generic breach-notification model, and the broad clinical-staff target population creates a larger-than-typical credential-compromise surface.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Healthcare sector pair (p50=$557,000, p95=$14,000,000); sigma=1.9602032156. secondary sigma inherited from primary; location scaled by analyst-judged secondary/primary ratio R=0.45 (HIPAA breach notification + OCR investigation + class-action ~45% of primary; regulatory tail for PHI breach is significant secondary component in healthcare)

name: Professional Services Payroll BEC — Direct-Deposit Fraud
description: Cybercriminals impersonate executives or HR staff via email compromise to redirect employee direct-deposit accounts to attacker-controlled bank accounts, or to authorize fraudulent wire transfers to fictitious vendors. Professional-services firms (law, accounting, consulting, engineering) are high-value BEC targets because of their client trust relationships, high-value payment flows, and relatively small AP/HR teams with limited dual-control over fund transfers. Loss includes unrecoverable wire transfers, wire-recovery fees, payroll-system forensics, and regulatory/employment-law exposure for misdirected payroll.
example_incidents: FBI IC3 PSAs document recurring law-firm BEC payroll fraud (multiple cases $1–$5M; Oregon-based law firm $2.5 M fraudulent wire). Accounting-firm HR email spoofing in tax season is a documented seasonal pattern. IC3 2024 reports BEC losses of $2.77 B across all sectors with professional services disproportionately represented by complaint value.
source_citations: IC3 2025 Annual Report p.7-8, p.132 (BEC per-complaint mean $123,005; downward-biased -- mixes consumer/SMB with corporate)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's SOCIAL_ENGINEERING archetype models credential compromise and data access; payroll BEC is a direct-transfer fraud where the loss is immediate cash disbursement (no data exfiltration), the vulnerability is in HR/payroll authorization controls rather than technical access controls, and the secondary loss includes the employer's wage-repayment obligation under FLSA and state wage-payment statutes (employees must be made whole regardless of the fraud) plus incident response/forensics and wire-recovery costs (FBI Recovery Asset Team success is partial) — a distinct secondary tail not captured in the standard data-breach secondary model.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Professional sector pair (p50=$736,000, p95=$17,000,000); sigma=1.9088254741. secondary sigma inherited from primary; location scaled by analyst-judged secondary/primary ratio R=0.70 (payroll BEC secondary: employer wage-repayment obligation under FLSA/state wage-payment statutes + incident response/forensics + wire-recovery costs ~70% of primary; misdirected payroll requires employees to be made whole regardless of fraud, driving secondary comparable to primary direct loss)

loss_form_profile.magnitude_basis: IC3 2025 BEC-specific per-complaint mean $123,005 (beyond-envelope own distribution; funds-transfer loss). Mean-preserving lognormal: mu = ln(mean) - sigma^2/2 so E[loss] = mean exactly; median = mean*exp(-sigma^2/2). sigma borrowed from the professional_services envelope (disclosed shape assumption, A1 parity).

name: Energy Utility Billing System Tampering — AMI/Meter Data Fraud
description: Cybercriminals exploit vulnerabilities in advanced metering infrastructure (AMI), the billing management system (BMS), or the energy management system to manipulate meter readings, billing records, or consumption data — enabling fraudulent under-billing, theft of service, or energy diversion at scale. Unlike physical meter tampering, cyber-enabled billing fraud involves unauthorized modification of digital meter data or billing records, with losses including unrecovered revenue, forensic investigation, regulatory reporting, and system remediation.
example_incidents: Cyber-enabled electricity theft-of-service and metering fraud are documented as an industry-wide non-technical-loss / revenue-loss pattern (state public utility commission proceedings and utility revenue-protection reporting); no single consolidated per-utility loss figure exists in primary sources.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype does not represent AMI/billing-system fraud, where the tampered asset is revenue-accounting data (meter reads and billing records) rather than operational or personal data, and the primary loss is unrecovered revenue (ongoing fraud drain) rather than a discrete breach event — requiring loss-period modeling distinct from the single-incident LM that FAIR's canonical form assumes.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Utilities sector pair (p50=$146,000, p95=$3,000,000); sigma=1.8377081683. secondary sigma inherited from primary; location scaled by analyst-judged secondary/primary ratio R=0.30 (billing system tampering secondary: regulatory reporting + customer notifications + forensic audit ~30% of primary; energy billing fraud secondary is regulatory + remediation class, moderate fraction)

name: Telecom Core-Network DDoS — Service Disruption and SLA Penalties
description: Cybercriminals launch volumetric or protocol-level DDoS attacks against a telecommunications carrier's core network infrastructure (routing nodes, DNS resolvers, signalling gateways, transit links) to disrupt voice, data, or messaging services across the carrier's subscriber base. Loss is dominated by per-minute revenue disruption, wholesale/retail SLA penalty payments, and expedited remediation costs including traffic scrubbing and capacity augmentation. Unlike edge-CDN DDoS, a core-network attack affects wholesale transit customers and downstream operators, amplifying the SLA exposure.
example_incidents: Lumen Technologies (formerly CenturyLink) BGP/routing disruption August 2020 caused global internet outages affecting major platforms (publicly reported; no per-org loss figure disclosed). A1 Telekom Austria 2021 DDoS on DNS infrastructure (publicly reported service disruptions). Telia Carrier backbone incidents (2023 NANOG reports note transit-provider DDoS patterns).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DENIAL_OF_SERVICE archetype typically models web-tier availability loss. Core-network DDoS has a structurally different loss profile: primary loss is wholesale revenue disruption per minute times the fraction of traffic affected, plus tiered SLA penalties from wholesale interconnect contracts and retail service-credit obligations — neither of which is captured by a standard breach-cost secondary tail model. The archetype also requires modeling the probability that scrubbing infrastructure is absent or overwhelmed, which is the key control gap at inherent posture. TEF counts network-disruptive DDoS events (sufficient to degrade subscriber-visible service or trigger SLA-penalty thresholds), not probe-level traffic; inherent posture assumes no scrubbing capacity to absorb such events.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no DDoS scrubbing center or traffic diversion in place
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Information IRIS (telecom→Information mapping, NAICS 51); IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Information sector p50=$718K, p95=$217M. Analyst PERT primary reflects revenue-disruption-dominant loss (per-minute downtime × wholesale traffic fraction × SLA multiplier), with high < p95 IRIS bound. No primary-cited per-org DDoS loss distribution for telecom core-network events found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued); secondary = SLA refunds + expedited remediation ~25% of primary.

name: Telecom SIM-Swap Fraud — Carrier-Liability Account Takeover
description: Cybercriminals deceive telecom carrier customer-service representatives or compromise internal systems to redirect a subscriber's phone number to an attacker-controlled SIM card. The attacker then bypasses SMS-based multi-factor authentication for the subscriber's financial and email accounts. Carrier-side loss includes FCC enforcement exposure, remediation costs, subscriber-facing customer compensation, and litigation. The inherent posture assumes no enhanced SIM-protection (CPNI controls absent or bypassable by social engineering against frontline staff).
example_incidents: T-Mobile FCC settlement September 2024 for $31.5 million (2021–2023 breaches including SIM-swap failures; FCC Order DA 24-925). FBI IC3 2024 reports 982 SIM-swap complaints totalling $25,983,946 in victim losses (IC3 2024 Annual Report, p.15). AT&T 2023 data breach related to subscriber data exposure included SIM-swap-adjacent carrier credential compromise patterns (publicly reported).
source_citations: IC3 2025 Annual Report p.7-8, p.132 (BEC per-complaint mean $123,005; downward-biased -- mixes consumer/SMB with corporate)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's SOCIAL_ENGINEERING archetype addresses credential phishing and deception to gain system access. SIM-swap fraud has a distinct carrier-side loss profile where the primary loss is carrier liability (regulatory enforcement, subscriber compensation, remediation of CS process failures) rather than data-breach costs. The vulnerability is in the carrier's identity-verification process for SIM changes (out-of-band human controls, not technical access controls), making the standard credential-phishing secondary model structurally incorrect for the carrier's exposure. TEF is campaign-level: one SIM-swap fraud campaign targeting a subscriber population is one TEF event; per-subscriber-swap-attempt frequency is NOT the TEF unit.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no SIM-protection flag or enhanced CS verification; identity check bypassable via social engineering
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Information IRIS (telecom→Information mapping, NAICS 51); IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Information sector p50=$718K, p95=$217M. Analyst PERT primary reflects carrier regulatory exposure + subscriber remediation (FCC enforcement $31.5M for T-Mobile for 2021–2023 multi-year breach portfolio; IC3 BEC per-complaint avg ~$26K for individual SIM-swap victims). High end bounded well below IRIS p95. Secondary = subscriber credit monitoring + litigation + CS retraining ~33% of primary. No carrier-side per-org SIM-swap loss distribution found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

loss_form_profile.magnitude_basis: IC3 2025 BEC-specific per-complaint mean $123,005 (beyond-envelope own distribution; funds-transfer loss). Mean-preserving lognormal: mu = ln(mean) - sigma^2/2 so E[loss] = mean exactly; median = mean*exp(-sigma^2/2). sigma borrowed from the technology_saas envelope (disclosed shape assumption, A1 parity).
citations: IC3 2025 Annual Report p.7-8, p.132 (BEC per-complaint mean $123,005; downward-biased -- mixes consumer/SMB with corporate)

name: Telecom BGP Route Hijacking — Nation-State Traffic Interception
description: Nation-state actors inject fraudulent BGP route announcements to divert internet traffic through attacker-controlled autonomous systems, enabling passive interception, traffic analysis, or disruption of targeted organizations and their customers. Telecom carriers operating transit networks are the primary affected entities: their routing table integrity is the target, and their legal/regulatory exposure, remediation cost, and potential diplomatic fallout constitute the loss. Loss at inherent posture (no route-origin validation or RPKI enforcement) reflects the asymmetric attacker advantage in BGP's trust model.
example_incidents: Pakistan Telecom 2008 BGP hijack of YouTube (globally propagated false route; publicly documented). Cloudflare + Telia BGP route leak incident June 2024 (publicly reported widespread internet disruption). China Telecom BGP incidents 2010 and 2019 documented in academic and congressional reports (Peking University/US Senate committee reports cite traffic diversion patterns).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype models modification of data at rest or in transit. BGP route hijacking targets routing-control-plane data (routing table integrity) rather than payload data, and the loss profile is dominated by legal/diplomatic cost, regulatory exposure (FCC/OFAC), and remediation of the BGP control plane — not data-breach notification costs. The primary loss is the cost of the incident-response cycle plus any government investigation or litigation, which is structurally distinct from a data-breach or ransomware LM.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure) route-origin validation deployed; BGP peering accepts unauthenticated route announcements
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Information IRIS (telecom→Information mapping, NAICS 51); IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Information sector p50=$718K, p95=$217M. Analyst PERT primary reflects legal/regulatory cost + BGP remediation + carrier-customer SLA exposure; high end ($25M) well below IRIS p95 ($217M) reflecting the more bounded cost of a routing incident vs a full data breach. Secondary = diplomatic/legal exposure + reputational remediation + RPKI deployment costs ~33% of primary. No per-org loss distribution for BGP hijacking found; highly asymmetric depending on whether traffic interception is discovered quickly or persists (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: Telecom Field Cabinet Physical Tampering — Wiretap and Service Disruption
description: Adversaries gain physical access to street-side telecom distribution cabinets (DSLAMs, fibre distribution hubs, cross-connect cabinets) to install passive interception hardware, disrupt service by cutting or disconnecting circuits, or clone subscriber lines. Loss components include cabinet repair and restoration costs, field-engineer deployment, FCC/Ofcom regulatory exposure for lawful-intercept failures, subscriber service-credit payments, and forensic investigation. Physical tampering incidents may not be discovered until a subscriber reports service degradation, extending the exposure window.
example_incidents: US law enforcement has documented multiple cases of organized-crime groups breaking into telecom junction boxes to clone business lines for toll fraud (FBI reports cited in Telcobridges industry bulletins). BT Openreach reported thousands of copper-cabinet vandalism incidents annually (Ofcom infrastructure report data). Physical wiretap installations at street cabinets documented in espionage cases (UK Metropolitan Police Operation Elate and US federal cases).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical-access threats to IT/OT assets, but telecom field-cabinet tampering has a distinct loss profile that combines physical restoration costs (field-engineer deployment to geographically distributed cabinets), subscriber SLA obligations (service-credit payments at scale for multi-subscriber outages from a single cabinet), and FCC lawful-intercept compliance exposure (carriers must maintain cable integrity under CALEA) — a multi-component loss structure not captured by a standard breach-notification secondary tail.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no cabinet intrusion detection, no tamper-evident seals monitored centrally, cabinets accessible with generic tools
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Information IRIS (telecom→Information mapping, NAICS 51); IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Information sector p50=$718K, p95=$217M. Analyst PERT primary reflects field-cabinet scope: restoration cost per cabinet ($20K–$150K depending on equipment type) plus per-subscriber SLA credits and regulatory exposure; mode ($150K) plausible for a multi-subscriber DSLAM cabinet with field-engineer dispatch. High end ($2M) reflects multi-cabinet campaign with CALEA compliance exposure; well below IRIS p95. Secondary = subscriber notification + regulatory reporting + forensic investigation ~33% of primary. Unbounded analyst PERT — physical cabinet tampering outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: Food Manufacturing Cold-Chain Ransomware — OT-Adjacent Operational Disruption
description: Cybercriminals deploy ransomware targeting food manufacturing and processing facilities (NAICS 311-312), encrypting ERP systems, warehouse management systems, and OT-adjacent temperature-monitoring and process-control networks. Cold-chain integrity depends on continuous automated monitoring; ransomware-induced downtime risks product spoilage, mandatory FDA recall triggers (21 CFR Part 117 FSMA compliance), and extended production shutdown. Loss includes ransom demand, restoration costs, product loss from spoilage, regulatory penalties, and supply-chain disruption costs to downstream customers.
example_incidents: JBS Foods May 2021 ransomware attack (REvil/Sodinokibi); $11 million ransom paid; US/Australian/Canadian meat processing shutdowns; CISA Advisory AA21-243A. Dole Food Company February 2023 ransomware attack caused temporary shutdown of North American production (publicly disclosed in SEC filing; no specific loss figure). Maple Leaf Foods 2023 operations disrupted (publicly reported; company confirmed cyber incident).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's RANSOMWARE archetype does not represent the food-manufacturing-specific regulatory tail under FSMA (21 CFR Part 117): a ransomware attack that compromises temperature-monitoring or food-safety record systems may trigger a mandatory recall even if the ransomware payload never touches the food itself — because electronic food-safety records required under FSMA cannot be produced during the outage, creating a statutory compliance failure independent of physical contamination. This regulatory secondary is structurally distinct from a standard data-breach notification tail and is unique to the food-processing sector.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no network segmentation between IT ERP and OT-adjacent cold-chain monitoring; phishing vector bypasses perimeter
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Manufacturing IRIS (NAICS family proxy, food-processing→manufacturing mapping per C-ii-b); IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Manufacturing sector p50=$1,000,000, p95=$42,000,000. Analyst PERT primary: mode ($2M) consistent with mid-size food manufacturer ransom + restoration, bounded by Mfg p95 ($42M); JBS Foods $11M ransom (single disclosed incident, CISA AA21-243A) provides TIER-3 order-of-magnitude context only, not a percentile anchor. Secondary = FSMA regulatory + recall-related costs + remediation ~40% of primary.

name: Food Safety Record Tampering — Malicious Insider ERP/QA Data Manipulation
description: A malicious insider with privileged access to ERP, laboratory information management (LIMS), or quality assurance systems alters food-safety records — passing contaminated product, suppressing test failures, or manipulating lot-traceability data — either to cover up an accidental contamination event, evade regulatory scrutiny, or sabotage the organization. Under FSMA (21 CFR Part 117) and the FDA’s mandatory traceability rules (Rule 204), falsified records can trigger both a criminal investigation and a mandatory market withdrawal, with loss components including the recall itself, civil and criminal litigation, FDA enforcement, and severe brand damage.
example_incidents: Peanut Corporation of America 2008–2009 salmonella outbreak: internal QA records showed management knowingly shipped contaminated product; criminal convictions resulted (US DOJ United States v. Stewart Parnell; 9th Circuit 2016). Blue Bell Creameries 2015: internal records indicated knowledge of Listeria presence; FDA warning letters + $19.35M fine. Jensen Farms 2011 cantaloupe Listeria: FSMA precursor enforcement. Note: these are physical-contamination cases with insider-knowledge elements, not pure cyber-tampering; they establish the regulatory-consequence framework for this cyber-enabled archetype.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING and INSIDER_MISUSE archetypes do not represent the food-safety-specific regulatory cascade under FSMA where falsified electronic records trigger mandatory recall and criminal referral independently of whether physical harm occurred — the legal exposure is for the record falsification itself, not only for downstream contamination. The secondary loss tail (criminal defense, FDA consent decree, brand remediation measured in years) is structurally distinct from a standard data-breach secondary and requires a food-sector-specific calibration.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no dual-control over ERP/LIMS write access; no cryptographic audit trail for food-safety records; insider access ungated
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Manufacturing IRIS (NAICS family proxy, food-processing→manufacturing mapping per C-ii-b); IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Manufacturing sector p50=$1,000,000, p95=$42,000,000. Analyst PERT primary: mode ($3M) reflects mid-case mandatory recall + regulatory penalty + forensics; high ($30M) reflects large-scale recall with FDA consent decree and class-action litigation, bounded below IRIS p95 ($42M). FDA enforcement precedents (Blue Bell $19.35M fine; Peanut Corp criminal case) provide TIER-3 order-of-magnitude context only, not per-org cyber-tampering percentile anchors. Secondary = litigation + brand restoration + FDA compliance remediation ~40% of primary.

name: Precision Agriculture Equipment Physical Tampering — Hacktivist GPS and Connectivity Disruption
description: Hacktivist actors with physical access to precision agriculture equipment (autonomous tractors, GPS-guided implements, drone-application systems) tamper with navigation receivers, firmware, or connectivity modules to disrupt planting/harvesting operations or to make a political statement about agricultural technology, corporate farming practices, or right-to-repair restrictions. Loss components include equipment repair/replacement, delayed operations (crop yield loss from mistimed planting/harvesting), and field remediation from mis-applied inputs. The inherent posture assumes no hardware tamper detection and open equipment access typical of working farm environments.
example_incidents: No major publicly-disclosed hacktivist precision-agriculture cyber-physical tampering incident found as of authoring date; the threat is documented in academic literature (Tippenhauer et al., GPS spoofing against agricultural receivers) and CISA advisories on agricultural sector cyber threats (AA22-137A). The John Deere right-to-repair controversy (2022 Massachusetts ballot initiative) highlights the attack surface; security researchers (Sick Codes / DEF CON 2022) demonstrated remote root access to John Deere equipment, confirming attack feasibility.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical-access threats but is calibrated for data-center or industrial facility assets. Precision-agriculture physical tampering has a fundamentally different loss structure: the primary loss is operational disruption (delayed planting/harvesting windows measured in crop-season days, with each day of delay having a yield-impact value determinable from agronomic loss functions) plus equipment repair — not data-breach notification costs or production-downtime at a fixed hourly revenue rate. The loss quantification requires a crop-yield-impact model rather than a standard LM model. Threat-actor attribution is prospective, not incident-derived: no public hacktivist precision-agriculture tampering incident exists as of authoring; the hacktivist pathway is grounded in the documented physical-access reality of the right-to-repair movement (mechanics and activists demonstrably access equipment firmware — e.g. the public right-to-repair jailbreaking demonstrations) combined with anti-GMO/environmental activism motive; analysts should treat the actor attribution as analyst-judged.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no hardware tamper detection; open equipment access in working-farm environment; firmware unsigned or update-unauthenticated
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — physical equipment tampering on precision agriculture assets outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus; no TIER-1, TIER-2, or TIER-3 per-org loss figure found for this event class (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued). Values reasoned from first principles: low ($5K) = single GPS receiver replacement + technician call-out; mode ($50K) = one or two autonomous tractors requiring firmware restoration + 1–2 days delayed planting for a mid-size operation (crop-yield impact at ~$300/acre for corn, 150-acre planting window delay); high ($750K) = multi-equipment fleet tampering campaign affecting a full planting season for a large operation. Secondary = regulatory reporting (if pesticide mis-application environmental incident) + field remediation ~25–30% of primary.

name: Agricultural Cooperative BEC Fraud — Grain-Transfer and Commodity Payment Diversion
description: Cybercriminals target agricultural cooperatives during high-value commodity-transfer seasons (grain planting and harvest cycles) using business email compromise techniques to redirect large commodity payments, input-supply invoices, or grain-elevator disbursements to attacker-controlled accounts. Agri-cooperatives are attractive BEC targets because of predictable seasonal payment cycles, large per-transaction values (grain lots, fertilizer bulk orders), and small finance teams with limited dual-control procedures. BEC campaigns commonly target predictable, high-value payment cycles; agri-cooperative payment volumes peak during planting and harvest, making those windows the natural targeting period (analyst inference).
example_incidents: IC3 2024 Annual Report: BEC is the second-costliest cybercrime category (behind investment fraud, $6.57B), with $2.77B in reported losses across 21,442 complaints (mean ~$129K per complaint). No specific agri-coop loss figure with a verifiable primary source found.
source_citations: IC3 2025 Annual Report p.7-8, p.132 (BEC per-complaint mean $123,005; downward-biased -- mixes consumer/SMB with corporate)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's SOCIAL_ENGINEERING archetype models credential compromise and technical-access pathways. Agricultural-cooperative BEC is a direct-payment-diversion scenario where the vulnerability is in invoice/payment-authorization controls, not technical access controls. The loss is the diverted commodity payment (ranging from a single grain lot to a seasonal disbursement), with low recovery probability (FBI Recovery Asset Team success <15% for wire fraud). The secondary loss is minimal because there is no data breach and no regulatory secondary tail — the scenario is pure financial loss with IR costs. TEF is campaign-level: one BEC fraud campaign targeting a payment authorization cycle is one TEF event; per-email-sent frequency is NOT the TEF unit.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no dual-control over wire/ACH authorization; email domain without DMARC enforcement; no payment-change verification callback
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: analyst PERT informed by IC3 BEC per-complaint average — an order-of-magnitude reference, not an anchor; IC3 2024 reports BEC mean ~$129K per complaint (aggregate $2.77B / 21,442 complaints, pp.9–10). Mode ($150K) slightly above IC3 per-complaint mean reflecting agri-cooperative transaction sizes (grain lots $50K–$500K). High ($1.5M) reflects a single large seasonal grain-elevator disbursement. Secondary = wire-recovery fees + forensics + credential-compromise remediation ~20% of primary. No IRIS sector row applies: agri-cooperative (NAICS 0115/5251) does not map to any published IRIS sector pair with a statistically sound σ; IC3 BEC mean is the correct order-of-magnitude reference per food_ag carve-out (MB-B2).

loss_form_profile.magnitude_basis: IC3 2025 BEC-specific per-complaint mean $123,005 (beyond-envelope own distribution; funds-transfer loss). Mean-preserving lognormal: mu = ln(mean) - sigma^2/2 so E[loss] = mean exactly; median = mean*exp(-sigma^2/2). sigma borrowed from the food_agriculture envelope (disclosed shape assumption, A1 parity).
citations: IC3 2025 Annual Report p.7-8, p.132 (BEC per-complaint mean $123,005; downward-biased -- mixes consumer/SMB with corporate)

name: Crop-Science IP Exfiltration — Competitor-Sponsored Theft of Seed Genetics and Agrochemical IP
description: Competitor-sponsored actors — including nation-state proxies acting on behalf of foreign agri-chemical firms — conduct long-dwell intrusions against crop-science companies (NAICS 325311-325320, 5417) to exfiltrate proprietary seed genetics, pesticide and herbicide formulation data, and precision-agriculture algorithms. Target organizations include major agri-chemical companies (NAICS 325311-325320) and agricultural R&D firms. Loss components include the economic value of the stolen IP (competitive advantage erosion), litigation costs (trade-secret misappropriation under the Defend Trade Secrets Act), and remediation of the intrusion. The long-dwell nature of the attack (months to years before detection) amplifies the IP exposure.
example_incidents: Multiple US DOJ trade-secret / economic-espionage prosecutions (2011–2019) have charged theft of US agri-chemical and seed-genetics trade secrets by China-linked actors, spanning insecticide formulation data (Dow AgroSciences) and inbred corn-seed genetics (Southern District of Iowa). FBI counterintelligence and DOJ prosecutions have documented sustained Chinese state-actor targeting of US agricultural and crop-science trade secrets.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)

LOSS UNDERSTATED: the dominant competitive-advantage / IP-theft loss channel is UNMODELED -- no defensible public magnitude source (adjudicated-damages sweep found none; the entry's prior IP cites are the gap-report mis-cites). Only incident-response + reputation are modeled here. Explicit sec6-materiality-bar waiver (plan-gate R11).
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_DISCLOSURE archetype is calibrated for breach-notification and regulatory costs, which are the dominant secondary for personal data. Crop-science IP theft has a structurally different primary loss: the loss is the erosion of competitive advantage from a stolen IP portfolio (measurable as R&D investment at risk, market-exclusivity period shortening, and royalty-stream impairment) rather than per-record notification cost. Trade-secret misappropriation litigation under the DTSA can produce large damages (disgorgement of defendant's unjust enrichment) but is highly variable and depends on successful prosecution — a fundamentally different secondary loss structure than HIPAA/GDPR notification.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no DLP controls on R&D file shares; no long-dwell detection capability; privileged researcher access to IP repositories ungated
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — crop-science IP exfiltration outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus; no TIER-1 or TIER-2 per-org loss distribution found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued). Crop-science companies (NAICS 325311-325320, 5417) map to Chemical Manufacturing or R&D sectors, not to any published IRIS sector pair with a statistically sound σ for this archetype (food_ag carve-out MB-B2 applies). Values reasoned from R&D investment at risk: low ($500K) = narrow trade-secret exfiltration, limited competitive advantage erosion; mode ($5M) = a defensible fraction of annual R&D expenditure at risk (major agri-chem R&D budgets $500M–10B/year; a mid-size crop-science firm at $100M–$500M R&D might face 1–10% IP-value erosion per incident); high ($100M) = catastrophic loss of a flagship genetics platform enabling a competitor to bypass 5–10 years of breeding program investment. Secondary = DTSA litigation + remediation + lost royalty stream ~40% of primary.

loss_form_profile.magnitude_basis: UNMODELED -- dominant IP/trade-secret loss, no defensible public magnitude source (beyond-envelope, sec6 waiver).

name: Hospitality Booking Platform DDoS — Peak-Season Revenue Disruption
description: Cybercriminals — including extortion-focused threat actors — launch DDoS attacks against hotel booking platforms, reservation systems, and property management systems during peak booking windows (holiday weekends, event weekends, summer/winter peak season) to maximize revenue disruption pressure and extortion leverage. Primary loss is direct revenue foregone during downtime plus expedited mitigation costs; secondary loss includes customer-compensation obligations, brand remediation, and potential OTA partner penalties. The inherent posture assumes no DDoS scrubbing or CDN-based absorption layer for the booking platform.
example_incidents: Marriott International experienced multiple DDoS-adjacent availability incidents (2022 Anonymous-affiliated disruptions publicly reported — a hacktivist instance illustrating the same availability impact). MGM Resorts September 2023 attack included booking-platform disruption (though primarily ransomware; availability loss was publicly disclosed at $100M+ total cost including reservation system outage — cited as TIER-3 context only; no per-org DDoS-specific loss figure available). NetScout 2024 Annual Threat Intelligence Report notes hospitality sector as a high-frequency DDoS target.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DENIAL_OF_SERVICE archetype does not represent the hospitality-sector-specific revenue-loss structure where booking-platform downtime during a peak weekend destroys irreplaceable perishable inventory (unfilled room-nights, which cannot be recovered once the date passes). Unlike a data-breach secondary, the primary loss is a direct revenue-foregone calculation (occupancy rate × room rate × downtime hours × number of properties affected), and the secondary is customer compensation (loyalty point issuance, OTA partner penalties) rather than notification costs — a distinct loss structure that requires a hospitality-specific calibration. DDoS against hospitality booking platforms is conducted by both cybercriminals (extortion, the dominant/primary actor modeled here) and hacktivists (protest actions); this entry models the cybercriminal-extortion variant.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no DDoS scrubbing center or CDN-based absorption; booking platform internet-exposed without traffic-diversion capability
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Hospitality IRIS (p50=$600K, p95=$62M); IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 Hospitality sector pair. The IRIS corpus is dominated by data-breach events; applying the sector pair directly to a DDoS availability scenario is an overclaim (breach costs != revenue disruption costs). Instead, the IRIS pair is used as an order-of-magnitude sanity check only: analyst PERT mode ($500K) is below the IRIS median ($600K), reflecting the distinct (lower per-event) cost of a DDoS event vs a data breach; high ($8M) is well below IRIS p95 ($62M), consistent with a smaller fraction of sector losses being availability-only. Secondary = customer compensation (loyalty points, OTA penalties) + expedited mitigation ~30% of primary.

name: Education Research IP Exfiltration — Nation-State Theft of Pre-Publication Data
description: Nation-state actors conduct targeted intrusions against university research departments and national laboratories to exfiltrate pre-publication research data, proprietary experimental datasets, and grant-funded intellectual property. Attack chains typically involve spearphishing of researchers followed by lateral movement to file servers and research repositories. Primary loss is the strategic economic harm from loss of competitive R&D advantage plus incident-response and remediation costs; secondary loss includes government grant-funding penalties and reputational impact on research partnerships. Attribution is predominantly Chinese, Russian, and Iranian state-sponsored groups per CISA and FBI advisories.
example_incidents: Nation-state targeting of US university research IP is documented across FBI/CISA sector advisories and DOJ economic-espionage prosecutions. University of California San Francisco June 2020 ransomware incident (publicly disclosed; $1.14 million ransom paid) illustrates research-institution exposure to destructive intrusions, though that specific incident was ransomware not espionage; listed here as context for the research-institution threat environment.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)

LOSS UNDERSTATED: the dominant competitive-advantage / IP-theft loss channel is UNMODELED -- no defensible public magnitude source (adjudicated-damages sweep found none; the entry's prior IP cites are the gap-report mis-cites). Only incident-response + reputation are modeled here. Explicit sec6-materiality-bar waiver (plan-gate R11).
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_DISCLOSURE archetype models the cost of data becoming available to unauthorized parties. Nation-state university research exfiltration has a structurally different primary-loss profile: the dominant harm is strategic economic value destruction (loss of competitive R&D advantage, pre-commercialization IP value) which is not captured by the breach-notification cost model that dominates FAIR secondary loss estimates. Remediation and response costs are the only financiable primary-loss component that has any statistical basis; the R&D value destruction is qualitatively large but not actuarially measurable per event. Attribution is prospective for any given university incident — the analyst-judged threat_actor_type reflects the population of threat actors documented across the education sector's research IP-theft threat profile (FBI/CISA sector advisories); individual incidents may have different specific attributions.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no network segmentation of research servers; researcher workstations unmanaged; no DLP or outbound data monitoring on research repositories
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Education IRIS (IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Education sector p50=$249K, p95=$6M). Analyst PERT primary reflects incident-response + remediation costs (financiable component); mode $350K above IRIS Education p50=$249K — consistent with a larger research department or multi-year campaign exposure; high $3M near IRIS p95 ($6M), appropriate for extended discovery of a sustained intrusion. The strategic R&D-value-destruction component is qualitatively dominant but not captured in financial loss distributions and is excluded from the PERT. Secondary = regulatory/grant compliance + notification + reputational impact ~29% of primary. Unbounded analyst PERT for the IP-value component — nation-state R&D IP exfiltration outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus.

loss_form_profile.magnitude_basis: UNMODELED -- dominant IP/trade-secret loss, no defensible public magnitude source (beyond-envelope, sec6 waiver).

name: Logistics TMS Data Tampering — Cargo-Diversion Fraud via Manifest Falsification
description: Cybercriminals compromise transportation management system (TMS) credentials or exploit weak authorization controls to alter shipping manifests, route assignments, or delivery confirmation records, enabling cargo diversion, fraudulent payment releases, or regulatory evasion. Primary loss is the direct cargo value diverted plus remediation and dispute-resolution costs; secondary loss includes regulatory penalties, downstream customer chargebacks, and incident response. The inherent posture assumes no anomaly detection on TMS record changes and weak segregation of duties between data-entry and approval roles.
example_incidents: FBI Public Service Announcement I-030318 (March 2018) documents cargo theft ring exploitation of freight brokerage systems and TMS portals for manifest falsification and load-diversion fraud. Port of New York/New Jersey cargo-diversion schemes involving compromised TMS credentials have been prosecuted (multiple US federal cases, 2015–2022, documented in DOJ press releases without specific loss figures per case).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype models unauthorized modification of data. TMS manifest-falsification fraud has a primary-loss profile dominated by cargo value (physical goods diverted) rather than by data-breach remediation costs, making the standard secondary-tail model structurally incorrect. The primary loss is the replacement cost of the diverted cargo plus the operational disruption cost of the rerouting investigation, not notification or regulatory penalties. Vulnerability is in TMS authorization controls (credential reuse, weak segregation of duties), not in network perimeter defenses. TEF counts fraud-enabled cargo-diversion events per year, not individual manifest-line edits.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — TMS credentials shared among dispatchers; no change-audit trail on manifest modifications; no anomaly detection on out-of-pattern route changes
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Transportation IRIS (IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Transportation sector p50=$490K, p95=$23M). Analyst PERT primary reflects cargo-value-dominant loss: mode $300K plausible for a single diverted full-truckload shipment ($150K–$500K commodity value range per TAPA Cargo Crime Report categories); high $5M well below IRIS p95. Secondary = dispute resolution + regulatory + TMS remediation ~27% of primary. No primary-cited per-org TMS-fraud loss distribution found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: Logistics Warehouse Physical Intrusion — Cyber-Assisted Access-Control Bypass
description: Cybercriminals exploit compromised access-control systems, disabled CCTV feeds, or cloned employee credentials to gain physical entry to logistics warehouses and conduct high-value cargo theft. The cyber component is the enabler (bypassing electronic perimeter controls) while the primary loss is physical cargo theft and inventory shrinkage. Loss includes stolen cargo value, physical security restoration, and downstream customer penalties for shipment failures. At inherent posture, access-control systems have no tamper alerting and CCTV has no integrity monitoring.
example_incidents: TAPA (Transported Asset Protection Association) Cargo Crime Report 2023 documents warehouse break-ins involving cloned access credentials and disabled CCTV as a recurring pattern in European and US logistics hubs (TAPA EMEA Incident Report 2023, aggregate statistics without per-incident loss figures). FBI reports on organized retail/cargo crime rings (FBI Crime Report 2022) document warehouse intrusions facilitated by insider cooperation and compromised electronic access systems.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical-access threats, but cyber-assisted warehouse intrusion has a loss profile dominated by physical cargo value (not cyber-incident notification costs). The cyber enabler (access-control bypass) and the physical effect (cargo theft) must be modeled together, with the TEF representing intrusion events enabled by cyber exploitation of access controls (not the underlying credential-theft events). Vulnerability is P(cyber-enabled access → successful cargo theft), incorporating both the physical access success and the probability of sufficient dwell time before detection. Unbounded analyst PERT — physical warehouse intrusion outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no tamper alerting on access-control panels; CCTV lacks integrity monitoring; no real-time access-anomaly detection; single-factor badge-based entry only
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — physical warehouse intrusion outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus. Primary PERT reasoned from cargo theft value profiles: TAPA reports high-value cargo thefts typically range $100K–$5M per incident for electronics/pharma; mode $400K reflects mid-tier warehouse cargo density. Transportation IRIS (p50=$490K, p95=$23M) noted for reference but not applied as a bound given IRIS captures cyber-financial incidents, not physical cargo theft events. Secondary = CCTV/access-control remediation + customer penalties + investigation ~25% of primary.

name: Competitor Trade-Secret Theft via Employee Recruitment
description: Competitor organizations recruit software engineers, product managers, or data scientists from a SaaS company with the intent of obtaining proprietary algorithms, customer data, pricing models, or product roadmaps through the recruited employee's knowledge and potentially misappropriated files. Loss includes litigation costs (trade-secret injunctions, discovery, damages), lost competitive advantage from disclosed IP, and remediation of the data exfiltration pathway. The inherent posture assumes no data-loss prevention on employee offboarding and no monitoring of file downloads by employees in transition.
example_incidents: Waymo v. Uber (2018): Alphabet/Waymo sued Uber for trade-secret theft by recruited engineer Anthony Levandowski; settled for $245 million in Uber equity (court documents, publicly reported). Epic Systems Corp. v. Tata Consultancy Services (2017): $940 million jury verdict for trade-secret misappropriation by a competitor-hired consultant (publicly reported, no per-org loss distribution derivable from single-case verdict).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)

LOSS UNDERSTATED: the dominant competitive-advantage / IP-theft loss channel is UNMODELED -- no defensible public magnitude source (adjudicated-damages sweep found none; the entry's prior IP cites are the gap-report mis-cites). Only incident-response + reputation are modeled here. Explicit sec6-materiality-bar waiver (plan-gate R11).
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's SOCIAL_ENGINEERING archetype addresses deception to gain system access. Competitor recruitment for trade-secret exfiltration is a structurally different threat: the asset at risk is people (the knowledge carrier), the attack vector is a job offer (not a phishing email), and the primary loss is litigation + competitive-advantage destruction rather than data-breach notification costs. The control failure is in offboarding processes and data-access governance, not in technical perimeter defenses. TEF is campaign-level: one competitor recruitment campaign targeting the employee population is one TEF event; per-employee-approach frequency is NOT the TEF unit. Attribution to competitors is prospective for any individual incident — this is the analyst-judged threat_actor_type for the archetype; individual disclosed cases (Waymo/Uber) may differ in actor structure. Attribution is prospective, not incident-derived in aggregate: most competitor IP-theft cases involve a corporate actor directing the recruitment, but public incidents also feature rogue employees acting without employer direction — analyst-judged as competitor-directed for this archetype's primary threat scenario.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no DLP on offboarding employees; no monitoring of bulk file downloads; no exit-interview IP-protection protocol; employment agreements lack enforceable non-compete with trade-secret specificity
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Information IRIS (technology_saas→Information mapping, NAICS 51); IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Information sector p50=$718K, p95=$217M. The competitive-advantage-destruction component is qualitatively dominant but not actuarially measurable. Secondary = ongoing monitoring + policy remediation + employee separation costs ~33% of primary. No per-org loss distribution for competitor-recruitment IP theft found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).
