name: Data Center Physical Breach — Unauthorized Access for Hardware Theft or Implant
description: Adversaries gain unauthorized physical access to a data center — via tailgating, cloned access badges, or social engineering of facility staff — to steal server hardware, install network taps or hardware implants, or destroy equipment. Primary loss includes hardware replacement, service-downtime revenue impact, incident response, and security-system remediation. Secondary loss includes customer notification (if data was accessed), regulatory exposure, and reputational damage. Physical breaches of data centers are rare but high-consequence events with loss profiles structurally different from cyber-breach incidents.
example_incidents: Multiple co-location data center physical-access incidents are documented in regulatory filings and court records without specific public loss figures. DXC Technology data center physical access incident (2023, publicly reported). CSO Online case studies document social-engineering tailgating attacks at managed co-location facilities as a recurring threat pattern.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical-access threats to IT/OT assets. Data center physical breach combines hardware-theft loss (asset replacement), service-disruption loss (revenue/SLA impacts from destroyed or unavailable equipment), and potential data-compromise secondary loss — a multi-component profile not well-modeled by a standard breach-notification secondary tail. The primary loss is hardware replacement + downtime, not notification. Vulnerability is the probability that physical perimeter controls (tailgating, badge cloning, social engineering of staff) are bypassed during an attempt. Unbounded analyst PERT — data center physical breach outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no tailgating detection; badge-based perimeter without biometric verification; visitor escort policy not enforced; no hardware tamper-seal program
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — data center physical breach outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus. Primary PERT reasoned from hardware replacement costs + downtime: server rack replacement $100K–$500K per rack; multi-rack destruction or extended downtime plausibly $1M–$15M for a large facility. Information IRIS (p50=$718K, p95=$217M) noted for reference but not applied as a bound given the physically-dominant loss profile. Secondary = data-exposure notification + regulatory + reputational ~25% of primary.

name: Branch ATM Physical Tampering — Jackpotting and Black-Box Attacks
description: Cybercriminals open ATM cabinets to physically connect external attack hardware (black-box devices or USB-connected controllers) to the ATM's dispensing mechanism, forcing the machine to dispense cash on command without a legitimate transaction. Losses are bounded by the cash loaded per ATM cassette (typically $20,000–$200,000 per machine per event) and the number of machines targeted in a coordinated campaign. Loss includes cash dispensed, ATM hardware replacement or repair, field-engineer response, and regulatory reporting costs.
example_incidents: USSS and FBI Alert (January 2018) and subsequent 2023 update document ATM jackpotting attacks using Ploutus-D and Tyupkin malware across U.S. financial institutions (advisory references; no per-institution loss figures disclosed). EBA Payment Institutions Fraud Report 2023 (p. 14) documents ATM fraud losses across EU payment institutions in aggregate; individual institution figures not published. European ATM Security Team (EAST) EAST Report 2023 documents 202 black-box attacks on European ATMs with €1.07M aggregate loss. EAST aggregate ~€5K/attack reflects all European ATM types including low-cash retail ATMs and partial failures; this entry models successful coordinated jackpotting of high-cash US bank branch ATMs where per-cassette cash capacity is substantially higher.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical modification of assets. ATM jackpotting has a loss profile tightly bounded by physical hardware constraints (cash-per-cassette capacity, number of machines in a campaign) rather than by data-breach-cost distributions. The primary loss is directly bounded cash dispensed plus restoration costs, not notification or regulatory penalties. The control failure is in ATM physical cabinet security (hard drive encryption, USB port blocking, cabinet tamper detection), not in network perimeter defenses. Per-cassette cash capacity creates a natural primary-loss ceiling not present in typical cyber-breach scenarios. Unbounded analyst PERT — ATM physical tampering outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus. TEF counts coordinated jackpotting campaigns (one or more machines targeted in a single operational session) rather than per-machine attack frequency.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — ATM USB ports not disabled; hard drive not encrypted; cabinet tamper switches not monitored; no ATM integrity-monitoring software (Wincor/Diebold security suite absent)
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — ATM jackpotting outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus. Primary PERT reasoned from physical hardware constraints: single-machine cash dispense $20K–$200K per cassette (industry estimate); mode $150K reflects coordinated multi-machine attack on a branch; high $1.5M reflects a regional campaign of 8–10 machines. Financial IRIS (p50=$1M, p95=$194M) explicitly NOT applied — those figures reflect all-incident cyber losses including ransomware tail; applying them would materially overclaim for a physically-bounded cash-dispense event. Secondary = hardware replacement + field-engineer + regulatory reporting ~27% of primary.

name: Financial Institution Transaction Tampering — Insider Unauthorized Value Transfer
description: A malicious insider with privileged access to core banking systems, wire-transfer platforms, or trading systems creates or modifies financial transactions to divert funds to attacker-controlled accounts. Typical methods include backdating transactions, altering beneficiary fields, inserting fictitious trades, or bypassing dual-control authorization by exploiting privileged access. Loss is direct financial (diverted funds) plus audit/remediation costs, regulatory penalties, and operational impact of transaction rollback and forensic investigation.
example_incidents: Société Générale rogue trader Jérôme Kerviel (2008): €4.9 billion in unauthorized trading positions; publicly disclosed in company statements and court records. This illustrates the upper end of insider-transaction-fraud magnitude at a major institution. Operational-level fraud cases (wire transfer diversions by bank operations staff) are routinely prosecuted by DOJ without specific per-institution loss figures disclosed in public sources.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype models unauthorized modification of data records. Insider financial transaction tampering has a primary loss profile dominated by direct financial (diverted funds), not by data-breach notification costs. The loss magnitude is bounded by transaction sizes the insider can authorize or modify — a function of their access tier and dual-control bypass capability. TEF is campaign-level: one insider fraud scheme targeting a payment workflow is one TEF event; per-transaction-modification frequency is NOT the TEF unit (an insider may make dozens of small modifications before detection — each is not a separate TEF event). Vulnerability is P(access → unauthorized transaction → successful fund diversion before detection), incorporating the probability of successful dual-control bypass.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no dual-control enforcement on privileged-access transaction modifications; no real-time anomaly detection on wire transfers; no segregation of initiation and approval roles for privileged users
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Financial IRIS (IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Financial sector p50=$1M, p95=$194M). Analyst PERT primary reflects direct-funds-diversion-dominant loss: mode $500K plausible for an operations-level insider with branch/department transaction authority; high $10M reflects a privileged-system-level insider (settlements desk, correspondent banking); high well below IRIS p95. Secondary = audit + regulatory + remediation ~30% of primary. No per-org loss distribution for insider financial transaction tampering found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: Healthcare EHR Record Alteration — Malicious Insider Data Integrity Tampering
description: A malicious healthcare insider (clinician, administrator, or IT staff with EHR access) deliberately alters patient medical records — modifying diagnoses, medication orders, lab results, or billing codes — for financial gain, revenge, or to conceal negligence. The loss profile combines direct financial (fraudulent billing, malpractice liability), regulatory (HIPAA OCR penalties for data integrity violations), patient-harm-remediation costs, and reputational damage. At inherent posture, EHR audit log monitoring is absent or not actioned, and the insider has read-write access to records beyond their minimum-necessary role.
example_incidents: Federal prosecutions of healthcare insiders altering records for fraudulent billing (OIG/HHS reports document recurring pattern; aggregate healthcare fraud statistics published annually, but per-organization loss distributions are not published). Elizabeth Holmes/Theranos case illustrates data-integrity fraud in healthcare at the organizational level (SEC/DOJ; specific per-incident loss figures in court filings). Individual EHR alteration by clinicians prosecuted by state medical boards and DOJ without per-case financial loss figures published.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype models unauthorized modification of data. Healthcare EHR record alteration by an insider has a primary-loss profile that combines financial fraud (billing manipulation), malpractice liability (patient-harm claims), and regulatory penalties (HIPAA OCR) — structurally distinct from the breach-notification cost model. The vulnerability is in EHR access controls and audit-log monitoring, not in perimeter network defenses. Loss magnitude is bounded by the scale of the billing fraud or the severity of patient harm, neither of which is captured in cyber-insurance claims studies that focus on breach-notification incidents.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no EHR audit-log monitoring or alerting on anomalous record modification patterns; minimum-necessary access policy not enforced; no separation between record-read and record-modify privileges for clinical staff
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Healthcare IRIS (IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Healthcare sector p50=$557K, p95=$14M). Analyst PERT primary reflects billing-fraud + malpractice-liability-dominant loss: mode $250K plausible for a mid-scale insider billing fraud scheme; high $5M reflects a sustained scheme with patient-harm malpractice claims; high well below IRIS p95. Secondary = HIPAA OCR + remediation + reputation ~40% of primary (regulatory tail elevated for data-integrity violations vs. breach). No per-org loss distribution for insider EHR record alteration found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: Retail E-Commerce Checkout DDoS — Revenue-Disruption Attack During Peak Season
description: Cybercriminals launch volumetric or application-layer DDoS attacks against an e-commerce retailer's checkout pipeline and payment processing APIs, timed to coincide with peak revenue periods (Black Friday, holiday season, major promotional events). Loss is dominated by direct revenue loss during downtime, cart-abandonment conversion impact, and potentially a ransom demand. Secondary loss includes brand damage, customer compensation, and expedited remediation costs. At inherent posture, no DDoS mitigation is in place and all traffic routes directly to origin infrastructure.
example_incidents: NETSCOUT Threat Intelligence Report 2023 documents retail-sector DDoS campaigns timed to peak shopping seasons as a recurring threat pattern (aggregate statistics; no per-retailer loss figures disclosed). U.S. federal prosecution of Lizard Squad members (2015) documented paid DDoS-for-hire against gaming and retail checkout systems during Christmas periods (court records, no per-target loss figure).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DENIAL_OF_SERVICE archetype typically models web-tier availability loss. Retail checkout DDoS has a primary loss profile driven by revenue disruption per minute of downtime (checkout conversion rate × average order value × downtime), which is highly org-size-dependent and not captured by static loss distributions. The vulnerability is in the absence of DDoS mitigation (no scrubbing center, CDN without DDoS absorption capacity) and the probability that an attack volumetrically saturates the origin. TEF counts checkout-disruption DDoS events (sufficient to degrade checkout conversion or take the payment API offline), not individual packet streams.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no DDoS scrubbing or CDN-layer absorption; payment API routes directly to origin servers; no traffic-shaping or rate-limiting at the network perimeter
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Retail IRIS (IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Retail sector p50=$746K, p95=$45M). Analyst PERT primary reflects revenue-disruption-dominant loss: mode $400K reflects 1–4 hours of checkout outage for a mid-market retailer (estimate: $100K/hour during peak season at 10K orders/hr × $100 AOV); high $10M reflects a sustained peak-day attack well below IRIS p95. Secondary = brand damage + customer compensation + expedited CDN deployment ~25% of primary. No per-org DDoS loss distribution for retail e-commerce found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: SaaS Revenue Outage Sabotage — Malicious Insider Service Disruption
description: A disgruntled or departing SaaS company employee with privileged infrastructure access deliberately disrupts service availability — by deleting production databases, disabling infrastructure components, or triggering configuration changes that cause service outages — causing direct revenue loss through SLA breaches and customer churn. Loss includes immediate revenue impact from downtime, SLA credit payments, customer remediation, and recovery costs. At inherent posture, privileged access is not revoked promptly during off-boarding and no anomaly detection monitors privileged-user infrastructure changes.
example_incidents: Cisco employee (2020) deleted 16,000 Webex Teams accounts and caused two weeks of service disruption after resignation; sentenced to two years in federal prison and ordered to pay $1.4 million in damages (DOJ press release, August 2020, filed as United States v. Sudhish Kasaba Ramesh). Rogue engineer at code-hosting company deleted production databases hours after resignation (documented in post-mortem reporting; no specific company/loss publicly attributed).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's INSIDER_MISUSE archetype addresses intentional misuse of authorized access. SaaS revenue outage sabotage has a primary loss profile dominated by business-interruption revenue loss (downtime × revenue rate × SLA penalty multiplier), not by data-breach notification costs. The control failure is in privileged-access revocation and infrastructure-change monitoring, not in perimeter defenses. TEF is campaign-level: one insider sabotage incident is one TEF event (a saboteur may take multiple destructive actions in sequence — these are not separate TEF events; they constitute a single sabotage campaign with an extended impact window).

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — privileged access not revoked at resignation; no anomaly detection on infrastructure-change commands by privileged users; no separation between infrastructure-change capability and production-data-deletion capability
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Information IRIS (technology_saas→Information mapping, NAICS 51); IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Information sector p50=$718K, p95=$217M. Analyst PERT primary reflects revenue-disruption dominant loss: mode $600K reflects a days-long outage for a mid-market SaaS (Cisco Webex precedent: $1.4M court-ordered damages for a 2-week 16K-user disruption — used as a plausibility check, not as an anchor); high $15M reflects a week-long outage with significant SLA exposure; well below IRIS p95. Secondary = customer remediation + SLA credits + reputation ~25% of primary. No per-org loss distribution for insider SaaS sabotage found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: Professional Services Office Physical Theft — Laptop and Storage-Media Theft
description: Criminals break into professional services offices (law firms, consulting firms, accounting practices) to steal laptops, external drives, or other storage media containing client data, financial records, or attorney-client privileged information. Primary loss includes device replacement, data-breach notification costs if unencrypted PII was on the stolen media, regulatory penalties (state breach-notification laws, HIPAA for healthcare consultants), and client notification. Secondary loss includes reputational damage, client churn, and litigation from affected clients.
example_incidents: FBI Uniform Crime Report 2022 documents office break-ins resulting in computer equipment theft as a recurring property crime category (aggregate statistics only; no professional-services-specific per-org loss figures). Law firm laptop theft incidents requiring state AG breach notifications are filed regularly in state AG data-breach portals (e.g., California AG, New York AG) without loss-magnitude data.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical-access threats. Office physical theft has a primary loss profile that combines hardware replacement costs and data-breach notification costs (if unencrypted data was on the device) — a hybrid profile distinct from either a pure cyber breach or a pure property loss. The cyber-risk component activates only if the stolen device contained unencrypted regulated data, making the data-classification and encryption posture the key vulnerability factor. At inherent posture (no full-disk encryption), every device theft triggers potential breach-notification obligations. Unbounded analyst PERT — physical office theft outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no full-disk encryption on laptops; no remote-wipe capability; physical office perimeter relies on standard locks with no intrusion monitoring; no data-classification policy distinguishing regulated-PII from general business data on portable devices
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — physical office theft outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus. Primary PERT reasoned from hardware replacement + breach-notification: device replacement $1K–$5K per unit; breach-notification legal + mailing costs $50K–$500K for mid-size firm with client PII exposure; mode $80K reflects 1–2 device theft with limited PII scope. Professional IRIS (p50=$736K, p95=$17M) noted for reference but not applied — IRIS captures cyber incidents, not physical theft. Secondary = client notification + reputation + litigation ~38% of primary.

name: Retail Store Employee Fraud — Insider Cash and Inventory Theft
description: Retail employees exploit POS system access, return-processing privileges, or inventory-management credentials to conduct cash theft, unauthorized refund schemes, or inventory diversion. Typical methods include voiding sales after cash collection, processing fictitious returns to controlled gift cards, manipulating inventory counts to enable shrinkage, or skimming from cash drawers. Loss is direct financial (cash/inventory diverted) plus investigation and process-remediation costs. At inherent posture, POS transaction audit logging is absent or not reviewed, and transaction-void authority is not segregated from cash-handling.
example_incidents: NRF National Retail Security Survey 2021 (reporting 2020 data) documents employee theft as accounting for 28.5% of U.S. retail shrinkage ($46.8B total shrinkage, NRF NRSS 2021 reporting 2020 data), equivalent to approximately $13.3B attributed to employee theft (aggregate industry statistics; per-organization figures not published). ACFE Report to the Nations 2024 (p.7) documents a median occupational fraud loss of $145,000 per case across all industries, with retail among the most-affected sectors.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's INSIDER_MISUSE archetype addresses intentional misuse of authorized access. Retail employee fraud has a primary loss profile dominated by direct cash/inventory value diverted (not by data-breach notification costs). The control failure is in POS authorization controls and transaction audit monitoring, not in network perimeter defenses. TEF is campaign-level: one insider fraud scheme (a sustained pattern of POS manipulation or inventory diversion) is one TEF event; per-transaction-void or per-refund frequency is NOT the TEF unit.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no POS transaction anomaly detection; transaction-void authority not segregated from cash-handling; inventory counts not reconciled against POS records; no CCTV monitoring of POS stations
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Retail IRIS (IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Retail sector p50=$746K, p95=$45M). Analyst PERT primary reflects direct-cash/inventory-loss-dominant profile: mode $80K consistent with ACFE median occupational fraud of $145K across all industries (ACFE 2024 p.7 — used as order-of-magnitude reference, not a per-org distribution anchor); high $1.5M reflects a sustained multi-location scheme; well below IRIS p95. Secondary = investigation + POS remediation + process redesign ~25% of primary. NRF aggregate shrinkage statistics used for plausibility only (aggregate totals prohibited as per-org anchors per C-iii research protocol §1).

name: Manufacturing Facility Sabotage — Malicious Insider Physical Disruption
description: A disgruntled manufacturing employee with physical and/or system access deliberately damages or disables production equipment, introduces contaminants, or manipulates process parameters to cause production shutdown, product defects, or safety incidents. Loss includes equipment repair or replacement, lost production output, product recall costs (if defective product was produced before detection), safety investigation costs, and potential regulatory fines. At inherent posture, physical access to production equipment is unmonitored beyond basic supervisor oversight, and no anomaly detection exists on process-parameter changes.
example_incidents: Vitek Boden case (2000, Australia): disgruntled contractor used stolen wireless equipment to release sewage from a Maroochy Shire wastewater facility; publicly documented in academic literature as the first confirmed case of cyber-physical sabotage by a former contractor (Slay & Miller, 2007, cited in CISA ICS references). General manufacturing insider sabotage cases are prosecuted by DOJ without per-case financial figures published publicly; aggregate FBI Economic Espionage and IP theft statistics do not sub-classify physical sabotage.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical modification of assets. Manufacturing insider sabotage has a primary loss profile dominated by production-shutdown costs (lost output per unit time), equipment repair/replacement, and potential product-recall costs — structurally distinct from cyber-breach notification costs. The control failure is in physical access monitoring and process-parameter change detection, not in network perimeter defenses. The threat_actor_type is insider_malicious (analyst-judged for this archetype's primary threat scenario); attribution is prospective, not incident-derived in aggregate — documented sabotage cases span contractor, terminated employee, and current employee actors. Unbounded analyst PERT — manufacturing physical sabotage outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no anomaly detection on process-parameter changes; physical access to production floor not logged beyond badge in/out; no segregation between process-change capability and physical equipment access; no continuous monitoring of OT-adjacent control system commands
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — manufacturing physical sabotage outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus. Primary PERT reasoned from production-shutdown economics: lost production per shift $50K–$500K for a mid-market facility; equipment destruction or product recall can reach $5M–$20M for food/pharma sectors. Manufacturing IRIS (p50=$1M, p95=$42M) noted for reference but not applied — physical sabotage outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus; high $20M reasoned from production-shutdown and recall economics for a significant but not catastrophic event. Secondary = safety investigation + regulatory + remediation ~25% of primary.

name: Financial Institution Call Center Social Engineering — Vishing and Pretexting
description: Cybercriminals call financial institution customer service centers posing as customers, law enforcement, or internal staff to manipulate agents into authorizing fraudulent wire transfers, resetting account authentication, or disclosing account information that enables downstream fraud. The institution's loss includes fraudulent transfer reversals (chargeback exposure), regulatory penalties for weak identity verification, customer remediation costs, and CS process remediation. At inherent posture, call center agents lack robust identity-verification tools and are susceptible to social pressure and pretexting narratives.
example_incidents: DBIR 2024 (p. 62) confirms Social Engineering is in the top 3 action patterns for Financial and Insurance sector breaches, with Phishing and Pretexting as the leading varieties. FFIEC guidance on telephone-based authentication (Authentication in an Internet Banking Environment 2005, supplemented 2011) documents vishing as a persistent threat to financial call centers. Specific U.S. bank vishing incidents are prosecuted by DOJ; aggregate FBI IC3 fraud statistics do not isolate financial-call-center pretexting as a separate category.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's SOCIAL_ENGINEERING archetype addresses deception to gain access or trigger action. Financial call-center social engineering has a primary loss profile dominated by authorized transfer fraud (the agent is deceived into authorizing a legitimate-looking transfer) and customer remediation costs — not by data-breach notification costs. The vulnerability is in the call center's identity-verification process (out-of-band voice authentication, knowledge-based authentication weakness), not in technical access controls. TEF is campaign-level: one vishing campaign targeting the financial institution's call center is one TEF event; per-call-attempt frequency is NOT the TEF unit.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no voice biometrics or knowledge-based authentication for high-risk transactions; agents trained on basic verification scripts only; no real-time transaction-velocity anomaly detection at the call center authorization layer; single-factor voice verification bypassable via pretexting
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: sanity-bounded against Financial IRIS (IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p.35, Financial sector p50=$1M, p95=$194M). Analyst PERT primary reflects authorized-transfer fraud + customer remediation: mode $200K plausible for a targeted vishing campaign resulting in one to several successful fraudulent transfers (per-complaint avg IC3 2024 fraud categories $10K–$50K; institutional loss per event higher due to aggregation across call center scale); high $5M well below IRIS p95. Secondary = regulatory + CS remediation + customer compensation ~25% of primary. No per-org call-center vishing loss distribution found (C-iii-b T2b negative outcome catalogued).

name: Education Campus Facility Tampering — Hacktivist Physical Disruption
description: Hacktivist groups physically tamper with campus IT infrastructure, power distribution, or access-control systems at universities — cutting network cables, disabling emergency-communication systems, or breaching server rooms — to disrupt university operations as a form of protest or ideological statement. Loss includes physical repair costs, operational disruption (class cancellations, research downtime), emergency response, and potential data exposure if tampered devices contained sensitive data. At inherent posture, campus IT infrastructure has no physical tamper alerting and buildings have limited physical security beyond standard door locks.
example_incidents: Multiple campus network infrastructure vandalism incidents at US universities documented in campus police reports and institution press releases without financial loss figures. Pro-Palestine campus protests (2024) included physical disruptions of campus network infrastructure at several universities (publicly reported in campus and regional media, without financial details).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's PHYSICAL_TAMPERING archetype addresses physical-access threats to IT/OT assets. Campus facility tampering by hacktivists has a loss profile driven by physical repair and operational disruption (class cancellations, research downtime, emergency response), not by breach-notification costs. The threat_actor_type is hacktivists (analyst-judged for this archetype's primary threat scenario — campus physical disruption with ideological motivation); attribution is prospective, not incident-derived for a specific university: documented incidents range from disgruntled students to organized activist groups to vandals, but the ideological-disruption pattern aligns with the hacktivist threat actor profile. Unbounded analyst PERT — campus physical facility tampering outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no tamper alerting on network-infrastructure cabinets; campus buildings rely on standard mechanical locks; no physical intrusion detection in server rooms or comms closets; no camera monitoring of IT infrastructure locations
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT — campus physical facility tampering outside the IRIS cyber-loss corpus. Primary PERT reasoned from repair and downtime economics: network-cable repair/replacement $5K–$50K per incident; access-control or server-room disruption with research downtime $50K–$500K; multi-building campaign up to $2M. Education IRIS (p50=$249K, p95=$6M) noted for reference but not applied — IRIS captures cyber incidents, not physical facility tampering. Secondary = emergency response + student/faculty notification + security upgrade planning ~30% of primary.

name: Tolling / Contract-Manufacturing Plant Ransomware — Customer Revenue Liability
description: Ransomware encrypts IT systems that orchestrate production lines operated on behalf of brand-owner or brand-principal customers — ERP scheduling, batch-management systems, quality-management systems, and historian interfaces that bridge IT and OT. Production halts for product the toller does not own. The loss falling on the tolling operator is the contractual make-whole: missed-delivery penalties, demurrage, reimbursement of customer raw-material holding costs, and lost-margin SLA penalties payable to the brand-owner third party. The toller's own revenue is a fee; the impacted revenue whose disruption triggers liability is the brand-owner's. Inherent posture assumes no OT-IT air-gap and no immutable offline backups for batch-management infrastructure.
example_incidents: Multiple contract manufacturers and chemical tollers affected by ransomware campaigns (Merck NotPetya 2017 — CMMC contract supplier disruptions; Brenntag 2021 ransomware affecting chemical distribution/tolling operations; general pattern: pharmaceutical CROs and food-contract-manufacturers have reported production-halt incidents attributable to ransomware on scheduling and MES systems).
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's RANSOMWARE archetype models primary loss as data-recovery and internal business-continuity costs incurred by the directly-attacked entity. It does not represent the contract-manufacturing / tolling structure where the legally liable party (the toller) owes make-whole / SLA penalties to a brand-owner customer whose product was not delivered — i.e. THIRD-PARTY revenue disruption, not the toller's own revenue loss. The asset under risk is the business process that generates the third party's (customer's) product revenue, not the toller's own revenue stream. Standard FAIR templates collapse this distinction into the attacked org's revenue loss, systematically under-stating the contractual liability tail.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no OT-IT network segmentation; no immutable offline backups for batch-management or MES systems; IT ransomware reaches historian and scheduling interfaces
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT anchored to contract-manufacturing liability economics: make-whole penalties typically 1-3x daily throughput value per contract day missed; tolling contracts for mid-size chemical/process plants run $50K–$500K/day product value; campaign outage 1–50 days implies a $200K–$25M contractual liability range. Manufacturing IRIS 2025 p50=$241K, p95=$9M noted for reference (attacks on the org itself, not the third-party-revenue driver); primary PERT deliberately sits above IRIS p50 to reflect the make-whole contractual liability multiplier absent from IRIS loss corpus. Secondary = legal/dispute resolution + crisis comms ~20% of primary.

name: Pipeline / Midstream Nomination-SCADA Disruption — Shipper Revenue Penalties
description: An availability attack — targeting either the OT SCADA system controlling compressor stations and valve automation, or the IT nomination and scheduling platform through which contracted shippers submit daily gas or liquids volumes — forces curtailment of throughput on contracted firm-transportation lanes. Shippers holding firm-transportation agreements cannot move their product and suffer direct revenue loss (basis differentials, opportunity cost, spot-market penalty) plus pipeline imbalance charges. The pipeline operator's loss is the contractual SLA make-whole obligation and firm-transportation-agreement penalty payments owed to those contracted third-party shippers. Inherent posture: no OT-IT segmentation between the nomination platform and SCADA; no backup manual nomination workflow; no diverse communication path for emergency shutdowns.
example_incidents: Colonial Pipeline May 2021 (ransomware-driven proactive shutdown; publicly reported ~$5M ransom; FERC/DOT noted shipper impacts); Oldsmar water facility 2021 (OT targeting pattern); broader CISA ICS-CERT advisories document recurring nation-state reconnaissance of pipeline SCADA environments (ICS-CERT Advisory ICSA-21-119-04 and predecessor advisories). Specific make-whole settlement figures are non-public.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's OT_AVAILABILITY archetype captures operational downtime and safety-incident costs for the pipeline operator itself. It does not model the contractual liability dimension where the pipeline's loss is the make-whole and SLA-penalty obligations owed to contracted shippers whose firm-transportation rights were curtailed — the shippers' revenue loss is the third-party driver, and the pipeline operator's exposure is the liability for that loss, not its own throughput revenue. This is a BUSINESS_PROCESS_THIRD_PARTY_REVENUE scenario (the business process being the nomination/scheduling service the pipeline sells to shippers), not a BUSINESS_PROCESS_REVENUE scenario (where the pipeline's own commodity revenue would be at risk).

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no OT-IT network segmentation between nomination platform and SCADA; no redundant manual nomination fallback; single communication path for compressor-station control
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT anchored to midstream contractual-liability economics: firm-transportation penalties on major interstate pipelines can reach $1M–$10M per curtailment-day for large shippers (basis differential + FERC tariff penalty provisions); multi-day OT outage implies $500K–$60M liability range for a large interstate operator. Colonial Pipeline 2021 reported costs ~$5M ransom plus operational costs; FERC enforcement precedent for firm-transport curtailment adds contractual tail. Utilities IRIS 2025 (p50=$146K, p95=$3M) is IT-incident corpus and does not capture OT-driven shipper-liability scale. Secondary = regulatory response + legal dispute + FERC coordination ~16% of primary.

name: Energy-Trading Settlement Platform Tampering — Offtaker / PPA Counterparty Revenue Liability
description: A data-tampering or availability attack on the energy-trading or wholesale-settlement platform (ETRM, ISO/RTO scheduling interface, or bilateral PPA settlement system) corrupts or halts the settlement process for power-purchase agreement (PPA) or wholesale energy contracts. The offtaker or merchant counterparty loses contracted energy revenue because metered delivery cannot be confirmed, invoices are corrupted, or settlement windows expire without valid data. The generator/trader (the attacked org) faces make-whole liability or dispute-resolution costs owed to the counterparty whose settlement was corrupted — i.e. third-party revenue disruption, not the org's own generation revenue. Inherent posture assumes no immutable audit trail on ETRM settlement records, no dual-path settlement verification, and no offline backup of metering data.
example_incidents: Ukraine power-grid cyberattacks (2015/2016 Sandworm — SCADA and control-centre attacks disrupting settlement-relevant dispatch data; ICS-CERT Alert ICS-ALERT-14-281-01B). FERC and NERC CIP enforcement actions for settlement-system access-control failures (publicly reported patterns). FCA (UK) supervisory notices regarding energy-trading platform integrity incidents. Specific per-org settlement-liability figures are commercially sensitive and non-public.
source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's DATA_TAMPERING archetype models integrity loss in terms of data-correction costs and internal operational disruption. It does not capture the energy-settlement structure where corrupted or halted settlement data triggers the attacked org's contractual liability to an offtaker or PPA counterparty for that counterparty's unconfirmed energy revenue — a THIRD-PARTY revenue loss that the attacked org must make whole. Standard FAIR DATA_TAMPERING scenarios assign primary loss to the org's own remediation and data-recovery costs; the settlement-dispute liability owed to a counterparty is systematically omitted. The asset at risk is the settlement business process that the counterparty depends on to receive payment for delivered energy.

calibration_anchor.vuln_posture: inherent (control-naive) per fair-cam-methodology 'Vulnerability anchor' — no immutable audit trail on ETRM settlement records; no dual-path settlement verification; no offline metering-data backup; settlement platform reachable from corporate IT network
calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: unbounded analyst PERT anchored to energy-settlement dispute economics: bilateral PPA settlement disputes and ISO/RTO imbalance penalties range from $100K (minor metering discrepancy) to $20M+ (multi-day settlement window corruption for a large generator or trading desk); FERC penalty provisions for market-manipulation or settlement-data integrity failures carry additional regulatory tail. Utilities IRIS 2025 (p50=$146K, p95=$3M) reflects IT-cyber incidents and does not capture contractual settlement-dispute liability owed to counterparties. Secondary = legal/arbitration costs + forensic audit + regulatory response ~20% of primary.

name: Physician-Practice Revenue-Cycle Disruption - National Claims-Clearinghouse Outage
description: A national medical-claims clearinghouse that a physician practice depends on for eligibility checks, claim submission, and remittance is taken offline by a ransomware attack on the clearinghouse operator. The practice's own systems are untouched, but the severed claims-and-payment channel halts revenue-cycle operations: claims cannot be submitted or adjudicated, cash flow stops, and staff divert to manual workarounds for weeks.
example_incidents: Change Healthcare (UnitedHealth/Optum), Feb 2024: an ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware attack on the largest US claims clearinghouse disrupted claim submission and payment for providers nationwide; an American Medical Association survey in April 2024 found most practices lost claim-submission and payment revenue and diverted staff to recovery.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no archetype for a business-continuity loss that originates entirely in a third party the organization depends on for revenue, with no breach of the organization's own data. The loss is a cash-flow/productivity interruption on a business_process_third_party_revenue asset, not a data-liability event, so notification/fines channels are near-zero and the magnitude tracks revenue-cycle downtime, not record count.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 healthcare envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare

source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)

Change Healthcare (UnitedHealth/Optum) ransomware outage, 2024-02-21 (ALPHV/BlackCat); AMA physician-impact survey Apr 2024 - ama-assn.org/about/leadership/hard-lessons-learned-change-healthcare-breach

name: Law-Enforcement Records Breach - CJIS-Adjacent Data Extortion
description: An external cybercriminal group breaches a state or local law-enforcement agency's records systems and, after encryption or exfiltration, extorts the agency by threatening to leak the stolen data. The exposed material - gang-intelligence databases, informant identities, internal-affairs files, officer PII - creates a physical-safety and litigation exposure distinct from an ordinary data breach.
example_incidents: DC Metropolitan Police Department, Apr-May 2021: the Babuk group exfiltrated and leaked about 250GB including gang-intelligence and officer files after ransom negotiations broke down. City of Oakland, Feb-Apr 2023: the Play group (and a later LockBit claim) leaked 600+GB including police internal-affairs and whistleblower-identity files.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's generic data-disclosure archetype does not capture the officer/informant physical-safety exposure and compromised-active-investigation harm that dominate a law-enforcement records breach, nor the outsized litigation/fines secondary tail from exposing statutorily-confidential records. Framed as CJIS-adjacent (state/local records that interface with federal CJIS data), not a breach of the federal CJIS system itself.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 government_public envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare

source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)

DC Metropolitan Police Dept / Babuk ransomware data-leak, Apr-May 2021 (washingtonpost.com 2021-05-13); City of Oakland / Play ransomware police-records leak, Feb-Apr 2023 (oaklandside.org 2023-04-05)

name: Integrated Resort-Casino Ransomware - Guest-Facing and Gaming-System Shutdown
description: A financially-motivated extortion group gains initial access to an integrated resort-casino through help-desk social engineering (voice phishing / MFA reset), then deploys ransomware that disables guest-facing and gaming systems - slot floors, digital room keys, property-management and payment systems - forcing a multi-day operational shutdown and large revenue loss.
example_incidents: MGM Resorts, Sep 2023: Scattered Spider used help-desk social engineering to obtain access and deploy ransomware; MGM's Q3-2023 SEC 8-K disclosed a roughly $100M Adjusted Property EBITDAR impact. Caesars Entertainment, Aug-Sep 2023: a parallel Scattered Spider intrusion resolved with a negotiated ransom (SEC 8-K, Oct 2023).
canonical_fair_gap: Existing hospitality archetypes cover card-data theft (POS skimming) and booking-platform DDoS, but not ransomware that disables physical guest-facing and gaming operations. The dominant loss is a productivity/revenue interruption from the operational shutdown, not data liability. The extortion payment, where paid, maps to the response form (recovery cost); it is not modeled as a separate loss form.

name: Telecom Lawful-Intercept / CALEA System Compromise - Nation-State Espionage
description: A nation-state actor gains persistent, credentialed access to a telecommunications carrier's core and lawful-intercept (CALEA) infrastructure - backbone and provider-edge routers and the case-management systems that hold court-ordered interception data - to exfiltrate call content and metadata for espionage. Remediation requires hardening or replacing compromised network equipment and reviewing lawful-intercept access.
example_incidents: Salt Typhoon (PRC state-sponsored), disclosed 2024: an FBI/CISA joint statement (Nov 2024) confirmed compromise of multiple US carriers to steal call records and data subject to lawful-intercept court orders; a CISA/NSA/FBI advisory (AA25-239A, Aug 2025) detailed the router-exploitation tradecraft.
canonical_fair_gap: Distinct from a criminal subscriber-data breach (PII monetization) and from BGP route hijacking (routing-control-plane tampering): the target is interception infrastructure, the actor is a nation-state, and the dominant loss is national-security incident response plus rip-and-replace equipment remediation, with no publicly disclosed per-org loss figure - magnitude is taken from the sector envelope.

name: Law-Firm Privileged-Data Ransomware - Double-Extortion
description: A cybercriminal group compromises a law firm and deploys ransomware with double-extortion, exfiltrating privileged client material before encryption. Beyond forensics and downtime, the breach of attorney-client privilege creates a distinct secondary exposure: bar-disciplinary risk, client conflicts and disqualification, and malpractice liability.
example_incidents: IC3 2025 Annual Report (p.16) reports Legal Services as the single largest non-critical-infrastructure sector for ransomware complaints (18%). Grubman Shire Meiselas and Sacks, May 2020 (REvil): 756GB of privileged client data exfiltrated with a large extortion demand. DLA Piper, Jun 2017 (NotPetya): a firm-wide outage with a lengthy rebuild.
canonical_fair_gap: Generic ransomware and MFT-zero-day archetypes do not capture the attorney-client-privilege loss channel - bar-disciplinary exposure, client-conflict/disqualification fallout, and malpractice liability - which drives a law firm's secondary loss. These are modeled inside the fines and reputation forms; no new loss form is introduced.

name: K-12 Student-Information-System Vendor Breach - Downstream District Extortion
description: A cybercriminal group breaches a K-12 student-information-system (SIS) or edtech vendor and exfiltrates student and staff PII across the vendor's customer base, then extorts the vendor and, separately, the downstream school districts. The districts' own systems are not disrupted, but FERPA-covered breach liability and notification costs accrue to them.
example_incidents: PowerSchool, Dec 2024: a compromised support-portal credential enabled exfiltration of student and teacher records across the vendor's K-12 customer base (tens of millions of records reported); the vendor paid an extortion demand and districts subsequently faced separate extortion using the same data. A criminal prosecution followed.
canonical_fair_gap: Distinct from an insider abusing district SIS access and from ransomware on a district's own network: the loss originates in a third-party vendor, liability accrues to the district, and the magnitude sits at education's own severity scale (not the financial-sector scale of the generic third-party-processor archetype).

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 education envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare

source_citations: IRIS 2025 Figure A3, p. 35 (sector loss envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare model)

PowerSchool K-12 SIS vendor breach, Dec 2024 (compromised support-portal credential; tens of millions of student/teacher records; vendor extortion paid; downstream district re-extortion 2025; criminal prosecution). Figures reported as a range across outlets.

name: Higher-Education Insider-Motivated Botnet DDoS Against Campus Network
description: An enrolled affiliate (a student), motivated by personal grudge, directs an external botnet to launch volumetric distributed denial-of-service attacks against a university's own network - central authentication, registration, and coursework systems - causing repeated availability outages during high-load periods. The attacker is an insider by trust/affiliation, but the DDoS is external and distributed, not conducted through granted network access.
example_incidents: Paras Jha, a Rutgers University student, ran a botnet DDoS campaign against Rutgers' central authentication and registration systems from Nov 2014 to Sep 2016; he pleaded guilty in the District of New Jersey and was ordered to pay $8.6M restitution (DOJ USAO-NJ, 2018).
canonical_fair_gap: The only insider-motivated denial-of-service archetype in the library. Distinct from external-extortion and seasonal-revenue DDoS entries (different motive, no extortion) and from physical campus tampering. Single well-documented exemplar (Rutgers/Jha); framed narrowly as insider-motivated external botnet DDoS against campus availability.

name: Judiciary Court-System Ransomware - Case-Management Disruption and Confidential-Record Exposure
description: A cybercriminal group deploys ransomware against a court system's own independently-administered case-management and e-filing infrastructure (a separate branch of government, not a shared municipal network), with double-extortion of confidential case records. Operations halt - docket backlog, missed statutory deadlines - and sealed or confidential records (juvenile, domestic-violence, sealed cases) may be exposed.
example_incidents: Kansas Judicial Branch, Oct 2023: a statewide ransomware attack took court systems offline for 5+ weeks with confirmed theft of case records. Los Angeles County Superior Court, Jul 2024: ransomware forced a multi-day full closure of the largest US trial court.
canonical_fair_gap: Distinct from generic municipal-ransomware (courts as one collaterally-affected service): the judiciary runs its own case-management/e-filing systems, and the harm includes a due-process/backlog productivity loss plus a fines channel from exposing statutorily-confidential records. Also distinct from insider records-tampering (external ransomware vs an insider integrity attack).

name: Edge VPN/Gateway CVE Exploitation - Ransomware
description: Cybercriminal mass-exploitation of a known CVE in an internet-facing edge appliance (VPN concentrator, remote-access gateway, perimeter firewall) as the initial access for ransomware deployment, requiring no user interaction. Distinct from the phishing/credential-derived ransomware archetypes that dominate the library.
example_incidents: Citrix Bleed (CVE-2023-4966) was mass-exploited in 2023 to gain initial access and deploy ransomware at organizations including Boeing, ICBC, and Comcast-Xfinity. Ivanti Connect Secure vulnerabilities (CVE-2023-46805, CVE-2024-21887, subject of CISA Emergency Directive 24-01 in Jan 2024) and CVE-2025-0282 were mass-exploited to deploy the SPAWN malware family (Verizon DBIR 2026 p.108).
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's MALWARE/ransomware category does not segment ransomware by initial-access surface. Edge-appliance-exploited ransomware has a distinct frequency (DBIR 2026's #1 and fastest-growing initial-access vector) and inherent-vulnerability profile (an internet-facing unpatched appliance is exploitable with no user interaction) versus phishing/credential-derived ransomware. Its per-incident loss magnitude equals a typical ransomware incident - hence deliberately loss-effect-identical to ransomware-on-fileshare (see _PL_ALLOWLIST); the distinctness lives in the vector and frequency (TEF/vuln), not the loss curve.

name: Edge Appliance CVE Exploitation - Nation-State Espionage
description: Nation-state exploitation of a known edge-appliance CVE to establish persistent access and exfiltrate data/secrets, with no ransom demanded. Distinct actor and effect from the edge-ransomware archetype: the loss profile is incident-response plus rip-and-replace hardware (replacement), with no productivity/business-interruption loss and no extortion.
example_incidents: Volt Typhoon, a PRC state-sponsored actor, compromised edge routers and appliances across multiple U.S. critical-infrastructure sectors for persistent access (CISA AA24-038A, 2024-02-07). Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect (CVE-2024-3400) and Fortinet FortiOS vulnerabilities were mass-exploited, and UNC5221 conducted espionage via Ivanti appliance vulnerabilities.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR does not distinguish nation-state edge-appliance espionage (persistent access plus secrets exfiltration, replacement-heavy) from criminal edge exploitation. This is a sector-agnostic archetype anchored to technology_saas only as a representative envelope, applicable across sectors.

Verizon DBIR 2026 p.15 (exploitation #1 initial-access vector). NOTE: cite CISA advisory IDs; CISA URLs 403 on automated fetch.

name: Internet-Edge Device Compromise - Foothold / ORB Repurposing
description: Compromise of an internet-facing edge device (EOL router/modem, VPN concentrator) as an unauthorized-access foothold and operational-relay-box (ORB) repurposing target. Direct loss is incident-response plus device replacement (modest); the downstream consequence of the access is modeled by separate scenarios. Edge routers are ISP/telecom infrastructure, so this archetype is anchored via telecom.
example_incidents: The J-Magic campaign compromised Juniper edge routers via the cd00r 'magic packet' backdoor (Verizon DBIR 2026 p.108). The FBI and CISA disrupted the PRC-linked KV-botnet of SOHO/edge routers on 2024-01-31. DBIR 2026 (p.34) reports 45,000-50,000 EOL internet-facing cellular modems with exposed management interfaces repurposed as operational relay boxes.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no archetype for an edge device compromised as a proxy/foothold rather than as the end target - modest direct loss, high strategic value as staging. Distinct from the OT-consequence scenarios.

name: Transient Cyber Asset - Contractor/Vendor Device OT Intrusion
description: A temporarily-connected engineering/maintenance/vendor laptop or removable engineering workstation bridging malware across the IT/OT boundary - the transient device itself is the vector. Sum of primary shares = 0.55 (not the ot_availability 0.90 default) reflects an access/staging event with partial disruption and no equipment destruction. Distinct from it-ot-bridge-compromise (network bridge via spearphishing).
example_incidents: This archetype is pattern-attested rather than tied to a single named breach: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS documents T0864 Transient Cyber Asset as a recognized top-tier ICS initial-access technique, and NIST SP 800-82r3 Section 6 addresses transient-device controls; the pattern of contractor/vendor-laptop OT intrusions recurs across CISA ICS advisories.
canonical_fair_gap: The single most-cited OT initial-access vector - a transient/contractor device crossing into OT - has no library archetype; it carries a distinct inherent-vulnerability and frequency profile from network-bridge OT intrusions.

calibration_anchor.loss_anchor: IRIS 2025 Figure A3 p.35 manufacturing envelope; Epic D-iii envelopexshare

name: Browser / Client-Software Zero-Day - Drive-By Endpoint Compromise
description: Opportunistic exploitation of a browser or client-software zero-day via a drive-by/malicious-ad delivery, compromising the endpoint (MITRE ATT&CK T1203, entirely unmapped in the library). Distinct from watering-hole-industry-targeted (nation-state, targeted site): this is opportunistic, cross-sector, and endpoint-first.
example_incidents: CVE-2023-4863, a heap-buffer-overflow vulnerability in the libwebp image library, was mass-exploited client-side across Chrome and other browsers in the wild starting September 2023. Google's Threat Analysis Group tracks browser zero-days exploited in the wild annually.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no archetype for client-software (browser/document reader) zero-day exploitation as an initial-access vector - a distinct inherent-vulnerability profile (browser patch state plus browsing exposure) from phishing.

name: Email-Client Zero-Click Exploitation - Nation-State
description: Zero-click exploitation of an email/mail-client vulnerability (no user interaction) - a distinct vulnerability profile from every phishing archetype, which require a click - used by a nation-state actor for espionage.
example_incidents: Microsoft Outlook CVE-2023-23397, a zero-click NTLM-relay vulnerability, was exploited by APT28/Fancy Bear against European government, military, and other-sector targets (Microsoft MSTIC and CERT-EU advisory SA2023-018, March 2023).
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR does not distinguish zero-click client exploitation (no user interaction) from click-dependent phishing - a materially different inherent vulnerability.

name: Removable Media - Air-Gapped OT Compromise
description: Delivery of malware across an air gap into OT via removable media (USB) - the Stuxnet class. Low inherent vulnerability (requires physical media plus an air-gap crossing), high consequence. The two existing 'USB' corpus entries are physical-tamper/ransomware, not this archetype.
example_incidents: Stuxnet (W32.Stuxnet, 2010) compromised air-gapped PLCs via USB media, per the Symantec W32.Stuxnet Dossier. The Raspberry Robin USB worm (tracked since 2021 by Red Canary and Microsoft) is a continuing example of the same delivery pattern.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no archetype for removable-media delivery across an air gap into OT - a distinct low-frequency, high-consequence vector.

name: OT Wireless / Rogue-AP - Field-Network Process Disruption
description: Compromise via OT field-wireless (WirelessHART/ISA100, plant Wi-Fi, rogue AP, cellular field networks) causing availability/process disruption - the T0860 class, absent from the library. Sum of primary shares = 0.50 (not the ot_availability 0.90 default) reflects partial disruption via a field-network foothold, with no equipment destruction.
example_incidents: Vitek Boden's Maroochy Shire SCADA radio attack (2000) is the canonical wireless-OT case, documented in the MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0860 procedure examples and the Abrams/Weiss NIST case study. The archetype spans rail, port, manufacturing, and water OT-wireless deployments.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR has no OT-wireless initial-access archetype; it is a distinct vector from wired IT/OT bridges.

name: Destructive Wiper / Pseudo-Ransomware - No-Recovery Sabotage
description: Deployment of destructive wiper / pseudo-ransomware malware with no ransom and no recovery path - the loss is extended downtime plus total-estate rebuild. Actor may be nation-state, hacktivist, or a false-flag pseudo-ransomware operation. An impact-axis archetype distinct from all 17 encryption-with-recovery (T1486) ransomware entries in the library.
example_incidents: NotPetya (2017, attributed to Sandworm/GRU) was pushed via a trojanized M.E.Doc software update and spread through EternalBlue/EternalRomance plus Mimikatz, striking Maersk, Merck, Mondelez, and FedEx/TNT. Shamoon/Disttrack wiped roughly 30,000 workstations at Saudi Aramco in 2012 and returned in a 2016-17 revival. Olympic Destroyer targeted the 2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics. HermeticWiper and WhisperGate struck Ukraine in 2022.
canonical_fair_gap: FAIR's MALWARE/ransomware archetypes assume an extortion transaction with a recovery path (pay, then decrypt). A destructive wiper has no ransom and no recovery - it is replacement-heavy (total rebuild) with no fines/ransom channel, a distinct actor and loss-form profile from encryption ransomware. Typed malware (destructive-malware) because a wiper is an availability loss, not integrity corruption (data_tampering).
